How about these examples?

#### Examples

Good:

```viml
augroup Foo
  autocmd!
  autocmd BufNewFile * put ='Nyan'
augroup END
```

```viml
augroup Foo
  autocmd! BufNewFile * put ='Nyan'
  " :au[tocmd]! [group] {event} {pat} [nested] {cmd} should be allowed,
  " because this command is a short-hand equivalent to the first case.
augroup END
```

```viml
augroup Foo
  " Comment node placed before autocmd! should be allowed.
  autocmd!
  autocmd BufNewFile * put ='Nyan'
augroup END
```

Bad:

```viml
augroup Foo
  autocmd BufNewFile * put ='Nyan'
  " autocmd! not found.
augroup END
```

```viml
augroup Foo
  autocmd! BufNewFile *
  " Partial removing should not be allowed.
augroup END
```

```viml
autocmd! Foo
augroup Foo
  autocmd BufNewFile * put ='Nyan'
augroup END
" It should not allowed because there are many similar cases.
" I want to keep allowed notations lean.
```

```viml
augroup Foo
  autocmd! Foo
  autocmd BufNewFile * put ='Nyan'
augroup END
" Same reason.
```

On Mon, Jul 4, 2016 at 9:23 PM, Rogério Paludo <notifications@github.com>
wrote:

> I'm working with big numbers, both integers and floats.
> The simple case of rounding towards positive infinity using ceil returns:
>
> >>> gmpy2.ceil(25852724578493228.946565)
> mpfr('25852724578493228.0')
> >>>
>
> Which is rater weird. I've tried to increase precision, up to ridiculous
> values; didn't solve the problem.
>
The problem is that the argument is is first converted to Python float with
53 bits of precision and then converted to an mpfr. You should initialize
mpfr instances from a string to get the advantaged of extended precision.

# With default precision.
>>> gmpy2.mpfr(25852724578493228.946565)
mpfr('25852724578493228.0')

# With extended precision.
>>> gmpy2.get_context().precision=128

# Note that even with extended precision for mpfr, the correct mpfr value
is only
# obtained when initializing from a string.
>>> gmpy2.mpfr(25852724578493228.946565)
mpfr('25852724578493228.0',128)
>>> gmpy2.mpfr("25852724578493228.946565")
mpfr('25852724578493228.94656499999999999999997',128)
>>> gmpy2.ceil(gmpy2.mpfr("25852724578493228.946565"))
mpfr('25852724578493229.0',128)

casevh

Running for example,
>
> >>> gmpy2.ceil(28.946565)
> mpfr('29.0')
> >>>
>
> is obviously correct. I'm using version 2.0.7 on Linux. I implemented the
> same code in C, and works like a charm with 5*1024 bits of precision.
> Master branch presents the same problem. May you please confirm what's the
> expected behavior?

Got something. While loop is innocent. In fact it never runs!

kernel.run_coro(self.run_co(servers)) never return.

That's where 100% cpu occurs.

```Python
class Tcpd:

    @staticmethod
    async def runservers(servers):
        for s in servers:
            await spawn(tcp_server(s.ip, s.port, s.handle))

    async def run_co(self, servers):
        await spawn(self.runservers(servers))

    def run(self, servers):
        # run(self.runservers(servers))
        kernel = Kernel()
        with kernel:
            kernel.run_coro(self.run_co(servers))  # <- Never returns.. 100% is here
            while True:
                kernel.run_coro(timeout=None)
```

HTTP (at least in my opinion) needs to be parsed as bytes from a stream of binary data (BytesIO) and then, once processed, decoded. I would say that it absolutely needs to be processed as bytes because of cases like gzipped response data, non-HTML responses like images, etc. For many pages, the charset parameter in the Content-Type header will tell you to decode it to Unicode, and for others it's in <meta> tags.

So the tricky part is that you want to parse, for example, UTF8 pages and get a Unicode result from dpkt. However, you might want to parse a .jpg response and get bytes back. So IMO there are two solutions to this:

1. Try to figure out the encoding and decode it yourself.
    * Pros
        * It Just Works for most users
    * Cons
        * More code, somewhat at odds with dpkt's lightweight ethos (or at least my perception of it)
        * Have to potentially parse HTML to fine those <meta> tag with charset, or at least grep for it
        * Potentially undefined and surprising behavior when a Unicode page doesn't provide a charset
2. Return bytes and let user code figure it out
    * Pros
        * Less work for dpkt
        * Avoids wasting cycles for cases in which people don't care, can work with bytes
    * Cons
        * Might be surprising if people expected a str out of the body field

In my opinion, dpkt's goal is to be a *protocol parser* and while HTTP fields can be used to specify the charset encoding, I don't think that performing Unicode decoding should be coupled with HTTP parsing, so I favor the latter option. A compromise might be a feature down the road in which users can set a `decode=True` to ask dpkt to search for a charset and decode if one is found.

Regarding the header values and HTTP fields ("POST", "200", etc.), you're right that it "feel wrong" and it certainly brings out the ugliness of the Python b"..." bytes literal. HTTP have this to say about headers:

>In practice, most HTTP header field values use only a subset of the US-ASCII charset [USASCII]. Newly defined header fields SHOULD limit their field values to US-ASCII octets.  A recipient SHOULD treat other octets in field content (obs-text) as opaque data.

([Source](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7230#section-3.2.4))

So IMO, the thing to do here is to decode the HTTP fields and the headers as Unicode ASCII encoding with the `errors="ignore"` argument. I will do that and submit a new commit to this PR.

일단 지금은 일을 쉬고 있는 중이고 lovit님 강의 열심히 듣고 있습니다.
제가 오픈소스에 기여해본적은 처음인데, 꽤나 유쾌하네요. 이래서 기여를 하나 싶습니다.

R features가 1개 밖에 없는 경우 제외하고 복합명사 인지 체크하고 이 체크를 위해 짧은 단어부터 검사를 하는 것으로 이해했습니다.

명사의 질이 좋아질 것 같습니다.  (다만 제외되는 어절중에 중요한 명사가 포함된 어절들도 있지 않을까요?? R features가 "noun_predictor_sejong"이면서 1개인 LR 그래프를 파일로 저장해서, 정성평가를 해봐야하지 않을까 싶습니다. )
 
그리고 { L : "안녕하십니", R : {"까" : 1000, "가" : 1}}의 경우 "안녕하십니가"는 노이즈로 볼 수 있는데, LR그래프 완료 시점에 노이즈가 있는지 점검해서 merge를 하든, 없애든, "가" : "noise"처럼 표시를 하든, "가" : "60%"처럼 노이즈일 확률을 나름 판단해서 추가하든 해서 LR 그래프를 재구성해보면 어떨까 싶습니다. 이 부분은 공부를 하면서 방법이 떠오르면, 테스트해보고 코드 검토로 의견을 여쭙겠습니다.

Just a quick note: all compilers override options listed earlier with those listed later, so your parsing of cflags is probably unnecessary as long as you make sure the user defined cflags come last of all the arguments, as these should always over ride any flags set by FoBiS.py. At any rate, explicitly parsing the user passed cflags and removing `-O0` added by `coverage` if the user passes a different optimization level will reduce the verbosity of the output and improve the clarity of what is happening, so I think it is a good idea, even though it is not strictly necessary.

Just FYI, I think there are more optimization levels that will escape your regexp: looking at the Intel Fortran Compiler’s man page I see the following additional optimization levels are available:

 * `-O`: Same as specifying `-O2`
 * `-Os`: Enable all optimizations that won’t increase the size of the compiled code
* `-Ofast`: Sets very aggressive optimization flags: `-O3, -no-prec-div, and -fp-model fast=2`
* `-O0`: No need to add an additional `-O0` if the user  explicitly requests it

It may be worthwhile to update the regexp to something like:
`-O[0-9,s]` to include the `-O0` and `-Os` flags as these are pretty ubiquitous across different compilers. But, as I stated above, things will always work correctly whether or not you parse the user cflags as long as they are the right-most (last) options passed to the compiler.

scheduler needs get from message queue and get once to check whether the task  is crawled then insert into database. So it's slower than just insert in benchmark.

Only one scheduler instance is allowed. And yes, you can separate the projects onto different servers (A project on A, B project on B) but can't share the same database table.

```

```
ldapsearch -x -h ldap-jaune.vdm.mars -b cn=schema -s base '(objectClass=subschema)' attributeTypes dITStructureRules objectClasses nameForms dITContentRules matchingRules ldapSyntaxes matchingRuleUse '*' '+'> schema2.log

apt install openssh-server

## Testing local
* connect to this local server using 
```bash 
ssh -D Portnumber_for_socks_proxy $USER@localhost
```
* connect to the open socks port using python
```python
>>> import socks
>>> s = socks.socksocket() # normal socket
>>> s.set_proxy(socks.SOCKS5, "localhost", 5050)
>>> s.connect(("localhost", 636),("localhost", 389))
>>> s.sendall(b"") # speak the ldap protocol with the ldap server
```
## Testing remote
* connect to remote ssh server
```bash 
ssh -D Portnumber_for_socks_proxy $USER@$DN
```
* connect to the open socks port using python
```python
>>> import socks
>>> s = socks.socksocket() # normal socket
>>> s.set_proxy(socks.SOCKS5, "localhost", 5050)

Hey @justiniso, thanks for the well laid out suggestion. this actually has been an issue I flipped back and forth on, and haven't really addressed why it is what it currently is anywhere (and also is still open to change for later versions). 

I am going to spill out my thoughts on the matter here, what I think the best solution going forward could be, and leave this issue open for a bit for discussion from others as well.

Currently Box v3 will raise KeyError when being accessed as a traditional dictionary and a key does not exist, and an AttributeError if accessed in dot notation. This is following with the idea that you know which method you are using when you write it, so you know which error to except (as it seems odd to `except KeyError` for an attribute). When I decided on this route I was trying to only use built-in errors to be as drop in replaceable to a dictionary. 

What I forgot to take into account with v3 is that there is now a custom BoxError, which means anyone using the library should expect that any errors raised by this library will be caught with that exception. 

My new train of thought is that a custom access error that is a subclass of all three should be used. 

```python
class BoxKeyError(BoxError, KeyError, AttributeError): 
    """Key does not exist"""
``` 

That way catching any of those would be valid, and would be backwards compatible. 

Thoughts?

This is interesting, TRAPs should always have boots/time initialized.

Keep in mind that boots/time are driven by the authoritative SNMP engine. The non-authoritative ones are supposed to store boots/time of all their authoritative peers indexed by their snmpEngineId's.

Your SNMP agent is running the authoritative engine ID so its boots/time should be the used in both SNMP TRAPs and commands.

Hi, I have pushed and published a new version with a fix for the bugs, which were blocking this scenario. There were a couple of problems:

1. When a context is saved, the engine raises an event, the mid for the event generated was colliding the the mids generated for this scenario.
2. The cleanup rule was not triggering in the redis script, so the redis memory kept increasing.

One suggestion, if in your test host you have access to your redis server you can also test your ruleset using the client (this is faster than http):

```python
from durable.lang import *

q = create_queue('daware')
i = 1
while True:
	print('Posting {0}'.format(i))
	q.post({'id': i, 'sid': 1, 'action': 'read'})
	i += 1

q.close()
```

Note: I have tested the scenario with more than 1 million events. I'm still not completely satisfied with the current scheme for generating mids for context events, as I use random numbers.

| `[0 1 2 3 4 5]` | `[3 4 5 0 1 2]` |

– both  list two triangles, and the `k`th node of the first triangle is connected to the `k`th node of the second. (As a consequence, the normals of both triangles both point in the same direction.)

However, one requirement of gmsh and your own code seems to be that the second triangle normal points towards the first triangle. VTU and the mesh generator you used don't seem to care.

Okay, I'm not sure how to best deal with this. Three possibilities:

   * You fix your mesh generator.
   * You fix your own code to get the wedge ordering right.
   * When writing gmsh, we make sure that the ordering is "positive". Not sure if I'm a fan of this since then the conversion vtu -> gmsh -> vtu would have different inputs and outputs. In general I would like to mess as little as possible with the data.

The orientation of the wedge can be computed by, e.g.,
```
dot(cross(x2 - x0, x1 - x0), cross(x5 - x3, x4 - x3))
```
-- I'm sure there's a formulation without cross products, too.

The python-gitlab docs section on [pagination](http://python-gitlab.readthedocs.io/en/stable/api-usage.html#pagination) mention this behaviour is to be expected:

> By default GitLab does not return the complete list of items. Use the `all` parameter to get all the items when using listing methods:
>     `all_owned_projects = gl.projects.owned(all=True)`

and

> python-gitlab will iterate over the list by calling the corresponding API multiple times. This might take some time if you have a lot of items to retrieve. This might also consume a lot of memory as all the items will be stored in RAM. If you’re encountering the python recursion limit exception, use `safe_all=True` instead to stop pagination automatically if the recursion limit is hit.

So maybe this is a matter of adding the `safe_all=True` to the GitLab `list` calls?

I've had a thought about interactions. As far as I remember last time I used gerrit (that was when I contributed some patches to some obscure Qt library), the process was:

- add the gerrit remote to my local repository ;
- create a new request on gerrit ; (or fetches one from gerrit)
- add the remote ref and pull it as a branch locally ;
- push -f my changes to that remote ref up until patchset review is approved and over ;

so how I'd see things, that would be:

```
# I got a bunch of commit to submit for reviewing
git gerrit add                  # adds the gerrit remote to the current local repository
git gerrit request create # creates and fetches a new request as local ref
git co local/refs/of/patchset/being/reviewed
# make changes
git push -f gerrit
```

or

```
# I got a bunch of commit to submit for reviewing
git gerrit add                  # adds the gerrit remote to the current local repository
git gerrit request list      # lists the requests being reviewed
git gerrit request fetch refs/of/patchset/being/reviewed # fetches a patchset to review
git co local/refs/of/patchset/being/reviewed
# make changes
git push -f gerrit
```

also, a nice to have feature, even though it's definitely not a must have, would be the `git gerrit list` feature.

*all()* seems to work differently when the field contains a list of, for example, strings than it does when the field contains a list of dicts.

With dicts, records for which the field's list contents exactly match the values provided in *all()* -- if the list in the record contains an extra dict, it won't be returned.  In this case, *all()* works as thoughtit means *exact()*.

But with strings, records for which the field's list contents include the values provided in *all()* will be returned -- even if the list in the record contains additional, unmatched strings.  In this case, *all()* works as though it means *at_least()*.  Example follows:

> db.insert({'name': 'user1', 'groups': ['user', 'test']})
> db.insert({'name': 'user2', 'groups': ['admin', 'user', 'test']})
> db.insert({'name': 'user3', 'groups': ['sudo', 'user', 'test']})

> db.search(Query().groups.all(['user', 'test']))
> \>\>\> [{'groups': ['user', 'test'], 'name': 'user1'},
> \>\>\>  {'groups': ['admin', 'user', 'test'], 'name': 'user2'},
> \>\>\>  {'groups': ['sudo', 'user', 'test'], 'name': 'user3'}]
Would you like me to drop 3.2 in this PR?

Hi tian-yi-ic,

Per the case you provided, if I understand correctly, you want to build a model of 1-lag auto-regression + constant trend? You can definitely fit such model using PyDLM. Following is a simple fake example.
```python
import numpy as np
from pydlm import dlm, autoReg, trend

# Generate fake random walk data using numpy.
n = 100
off_set = 0
y = [0] * n
y[0] = off_set + np.random.normal() * 2
for i in range(1, n):
  constant = off_set + np.random.normal() * 2
  y[i] = y[i - 1] + constant + np.random.normal()

# Build model with pydlm
myDLM = dlm(y) + trend(degree=0, discount=0.98) + autoReg(degree=1, data=y, discount=1.0)
myDLM.fit()
myDLM.plot()

```

Thanks for reporting this and the example. I'm actually undecided whether this is a bug or not.

(Below is some thinking aloud in case you have further suggestions.
TL;DR: I will add an `as_text` argument to `saveas()`)

---

This issue is related to #110 and #145, where output from BEDTools programs results in non-BED-format files. Sending that incorrectly-formatted file to other tools expecting BED-like input raises an error, as it should.

One "tool" expecting BED-like input is iterating over a `BedTool` object. Since the iterator is designed to yield `Interval` objects, we get errors when it can't create one due to non-BED format. Hence #145.

For this issue #146, `saveas()` is simply calling the iterator, running into similar problems.

Options around this:
1. leave things as-is
2. give `saveas()` a new argument -- maybe `as_text`? -- that just dumps to file without making an iterator out of things. Then using `.saveas(as_text=True)` would solve your issue here, but not ones like #145.
3. have `BedTool` objects be smarter about what they should yield when iterating. If creating an `Interval` out of the first line works, then yield `Interval` objects from then on; otherwise just yield lines. Or lines split on tabs maybe.


Option 1 is OK (of course I'd add more docs about it, and there should really be an FAQ entry on this topic).

Option 2 seems reasonable. While it wouldn't solve #145, it would make the solution to #145 cleaner. That is, you wouldn't have to treat the `BedTool.fn` differently depending on whether it was streaming or file-based.

Option 3 is not very explicit and is likely to create more confusion since the kind of object yielded is not consistent.

So . . . I'll add a new argument to `saveas()`.

I steadfastly refuse to describe any conventions related to rotations in terms of names like "classic Euler" or "Tait-Bryan" because I have literally never seen a complete description of any such term — which has led me to find directly contradictory definitions for most such terms.  For example, people go as far as defining the rotation operators, but not how those operators are applied, and then different people inexplicably have different interpretations of how they should be applied.  I've also seen at least three distinct and contradictory definitions of the phrase "active vs. passive".

Instead, I think the only useful description is the one I give on [the page linked from that footnote](http://moble.github.io/spherical_functions/#euler-angles) where I complain about Euler angles.  More specifically, you can test for yourself that in this code
```python
quaternion.from_euler_angles(alpha, beta, gamma)
```
is the same as
```python
(alpha*quaternion.z/2).exp()*(beta*quaternion.y/2).exp()*(gamma*quaternion.z/2).exp()
```
Of course, even with that definition, I've seen people define that quantity as `R` and people define that as `R` inverse — and then in each of those two camps, I've seen people define a rotated vector to be `R*v*R^-1` and people define it to be `R^-1 * v * R`.  This package doesn't restrict you to one choice or the other in either case.  You have to figure out what conventions you want to use.

Similarly, you can see that
```python
quaternion(0, 1, 0, 0) * quaternion(0, 0, 1, 0)
```
is the same as
```python
quaternion(0, 0, 0, 1)
```
So `i*j = k` would be more like this package's conventions — depending on how you interpret lots of other things.

My advice is to test write unit tests for whatever code you're writing to ensure that known inputs give you the results you expect at each step of the process.  Having written much code myself, I can tell you that's the only thing that works.  Never rely on anyone else's tidy little names for their conventions.

Docs are updated:

IPRoute: http://docs.pyroute2.org/iproute.html#pyroute2.iproute.IPRouteMixin.route
IPDB: http://docs.pyroute2.org/ipdb.html#routing-management

```
from pyroute2 import IPRoute

ipr = IPRoute()
ipr.get_routes(dst='8.8.8.8')
ipr.close()
```

or

```
from pyroute2 import IPDB
ipdb = IPDB()
ipdb.routes[spec]
```
If `spec` points to an existing route, the route record will be returned, otherwise will be returned the same as for `ip route get`.

The `spec` can be a string (prefix) or a dict, e.g.:
```
ipdb.routes['8.8.8.8']
ipdb.routes[{'dst': '172.16.0.0/24', 'oif': 2}]
```
the latter can be used when there are several routes with the same prefix.

IPDB is faster not 'cause of batch requests — it does not employ batch requests yet, the feature is planned; but batch requests don't make the work much faster, though.

The main reason, why it is faster, is that it doesn't look up all the system info every time it makes a request. Look, an example:
```
# iproute command
$ ip addr add 10.0.0.2/24 dev eth0

# under the hood
# 1. get all the interfaces info
type: RTM_GETLINK, flags: |NLM_F_DUMP → all the interfaces info (a HUGE load of messages)
# 2. resolve the interface name «eth0»
# 3. add the address
type: RTM_NEWADDR → RTM_NEWADDR (response)
```

But for IPDB the workflow differs a lot:
```
# the code
with ipdb.interfaces['eth0'] as i:
    i.add_ip('10.0.0.2/24')

# under the hood
# 1. resolve the interface via cached names (no netlink requests)
# 2. add the address
type: RTM_NEWADDR → RTM_NEWADDR (response + broadcast)
# 3. update the DB with the broadcasted information
```

When you work on hundreds of the network objects like interfaces or routes, IPDB just collects the broadcasted information, it makes no dumps (almost ;), it saves a lot of CPU resources just not requesting what's known already.

So you can use IPRoute together with IPDB to add the TC objects, using IPDB as the cache for interface names, address, routes etc.

If you will need a TC cache, you will have to implement it yourself. But there is an issue.

Sync with RTNL broadcasts:
```
→ request RTM_NEWADDR
← response RTM_NEWADDR
# but the address may be NOT ready yet!
← broadcast RTM_NEWADDR
# here the address IS ready to use
```

IPDB doesn't rely solely on responses, it waits for broadcasts, and it is a relatively reliable way to keep cache in sync and have no races. 

With TC objects everything differs:
```
→ request RTM_NEWQDISC
← response RTM_NEWQDISC
# the qdisc may be NOT ready
# but there is no broadcasts
loop:
    → request RTM_GETQDISC, flags: |NLM_F_DUMP
    ← response RTM_NEWQDISC, a list of all qdisc objects
    # look up if the qdisc appeared in the list, otherwise repeat
```

That loop with qdisc lookups kills all the performance. If in your case it is not important to wait until the qdisc appears, you can avoid the sync step, but IPDB would not: it has to guarantee the system state after the `commit()` call.

Hello!

The ``public`` parameter is used to hide APIs from the generated specification according to the permissions of the user who views the API documentation page. With ``public=True``, anyone who has access to view the swagger page can see ALL endpoints in the api, while with ``False``, they would only see the ones they can actually call according to their current credentials.

If you want to restrict who has access to the page itself, that would be achived, as with any regular view, via ``permission_classes``. The ``get_schema_view`` method also provides it as a convenience argument, so you could achieve what you want simply by replacing ``permission_classes=(permissions.AllowAny,), `` with, say, ``permission_classes=(permissions.IsAuthenticated,),``, or whatever floats your boat.

The main thing to take away is that the ``permission_classes`` on the swagger view are unrelated to the ``public`` argument to the spec generator - one controls who can VIEW the spec, while the other controls what endpoints are included in it.

On Thu, Jan 14, 2016 at 8:46 PM, Mike Jarvis <notifications@github.com>
wrote:

> A couple things here. First, the internal units for all angles in TreeCorr
> is radians, so accessing cat_data.ra[i] and cat_data_dec[i] will show the
> value in radians, not degrees. That explains the factor of 57 you saw.
>
> The bigger issue you are seeing is that the reported separations for the
> final correlation function are technically the chord distances across the
> unit sphere rather than the great circle angle. cf. the documentation of
> the Euclidean metric
> <http://rmjarvis.github.io/TreeCorr/html/nn.html#treecorr.NNCorrelation.process>,
> although I admit the documentation about this fact is probably too light
> currently.
>
> For small angles, they are basically equivalent, so it doesn't make much
> difference. But with angles of 90 or 180 degrees, it makes a bit
> difference. An angle of 180 degrees is pi radians. But the chord distance
> is only 2 (the diameter). If you ask for this in degrees, it will multiply
> by 180/pi to get 115, not 180. With the binning, I think this is why it
> looked like a maximum of 128 degrees. Probably it was really a maximum of
> 115 "degrees", which means a maximum of 2 for the chord distances.
>
> The reason for this is that it is much faster to compute distances in
> Euclidean coordinates rather than in spherical coordinates, so we put all
> the points on the unit sphere and then use Euclidean separations for
> everything. I should probably change this so that it at least converts back
> to the great circle distance at the end, but I haven't yet, since most
> people are using separations < 10 degrees where it doesn't make a big
> difference.
>
> In the meanwhile, you can convert the reported distances yourself using
> the formula:
>
> r_GC = 2 * math.asin(0.5 * r_chord)
>
> where both r_GC and r_chord are in radians.
>
> —
> Reply to this email directly or view it on GitHub
> <https://github.com/rmjarvis/TreeCorr/issues/39#issuecomment-171635385>.
>

Hi, @Mottie!
It looks I wrote a bad description of changes I've made. Let me try to explain it more concrete. All points you've listed were taken into account.
I made no changes in `c.$tbodies` definition. Quite the contrary, I've changed the definition of `c.cache` so now both variables do **not** include info only tbodies. Now `c.cache[tbodyIndex]` and `c.$tbodies[tbodyIndex]` both refer the same non-infoOnly tbody. So I don't get into issues you've mentioned.
There was no one place where cache for info only tbodies is used, so I think it's safe to remove unnecessary empty cache entries for those tbodies. Workaround which were used to access right cache element are now removed because we automatically get right cache indices. But even if they weren't removed, actually they would work anyway (typically they iterate through a list of all tbodies to find specific one non-infoOnly tbody; while index obtained this way can be different after patch, the tbody found is quite the same). So if somebody have written his own widget using approaches same as used in tablesorter itself, their widgets also don't need any changes. Also if one do not use `c.cache`, he also shouldn't bother.

The only situation which can be broken - if someone had manually iterated cache indices through all tbodies using 
`for(i = 0; i < c.$table.children('tbody').length; ++i) {...}`
So yes, it can break compatibility in rare cases. But the library itself is consistent.

I've checked and fixed all the widgets in a project that they use `c.$tbodies` and `c.cache` in a proper way. Also I've tried to fix unit tests to check updated cache. Did you see the second commit in this pull request (I pushed it not immediately after the first commit)?

I understand the implementation is rather naive and if you look closely, it's obvious why you made these choices. I'm trying to point out that there are ways that will come to haunt you if you use this in production. Consider the following example:
```
class Something(object):
    @memoize
    def expensive_object(self):
        return generate_expensive_object()
```

Reading this code (without knowing what memoize does), I would expect that the expensive object is cleaned up after a "Something" object goes out of scope. But it does not.
```
a = Something().expensive_object()
b = Something().expensive_object()
c = Something().expensive_object()

del a
del b
del c
```

Now there's three expensive objects and three Something objects still in existence.

There are different scan values for documents examined versus index entries. The mtools names are based on historical parameters in MongoDB explain/log results:

- `nscanned` is the number of index entries (aka [`totalKeyExamined`](https://docs.mongodb.com/manual/reference/explain-results/#explain.executionStats.totalKeysExamined) in modern MongoDB explain results)

- `nscannedObjects` is the number of documents examined (aka [`totalDocsExamined`](https://docs.mongodb.com/manual/reference/explain-results/#explain.executionStats.totalDocsExamined) in modern MongoDB explain results)

There is also `n` (aka [`nReturned`](https://docs.mongodb.com/manual/reference/explain-results/#explain.executionStats.nReturned) in modern explain results) which is the number of matching documents. A typical query for index efficiency is comparing the ratio of `nscanned/n` (for example `mplotqueries --type nscanned/n ...`).

The `mlogvis` grouping option for `nscanned` is currently only showing index key scans. It would be more useful to replace this with `nscanned/n`, but `mlogvis` is currently a simple visualisation without any derived metrics.

Is there a reason you are unable to use the full `mplotqueries`?

If you are only interested in collection scans or inefficient queries you can filter the logs before visualising:

- `mlogfilter mongod.log --planSummary COLLSCAN | mlogvis` # plan summary indicates collection scan
- `mlogfilter mongodb.log --scan | mlogvis`  # only output lines with [poor index usage](http://blog.rueckstiess.com/mtools/mlogfilter.html#collection-scans)

Regards,

Hi efialtisgr, I'm glad you are finding Pyrebase useful!

There are a couple of ways to approach this problem. 

The first is to set up an [index](https://firebase.google.com/docs/database/security/indexing-data#defining_data_indexes) in your security rules to allow for complex queries on specific properties in your database. In this example we'll be querying the ```match``` property, so our security rules might look something like this:
```
{
  "rules": {
    ".read": "true",
    ".write": "true",
    "xtrmp": {
      ".indexOn": ["match"]
    }
  }
}
```
Now we can run a query to check if a child with the ```match``` type of "FC BULLEEN LIONS vs. SOUTH MELBOURNE" exists, for example:
```
db = firebase.database()
matches = db.child("xtrmp").order_by_child("match").equal_to("FC BULLEEN LIONS vs. SOUTH MELBOURNE").get(user["idToken"])
if not matches.val():
    # data doesn't exist, we now know can push the data
```

That is one way to solve this. 

Another way to solve this problem is to save your data with custom made keys, rather than keys generated by firebase. Let's pretend we save all our matches with keys that follow the naming scheme of ```"TEAMvsTEAM"```, for example ```"BLvsSM"```:
```
db.child("xtrmp").child("BLvsSM").set(data, user["idToken"])
```
Now we'll be able to easily check if data exists in these locations, for example:
```
match = db.child("xtrmp").child("BLvsSM").get(user["idToken"])
if not match.val():
    # data doesn't exist, we now know can push the data
```

Hi. I'm revisiting this... I think using `{state}` or `{icon}` is the wrong approach when it comes to customizing. AFAIK you may have a branch that allows more rich colors and we wouldn't be able to apply it here without this PR or other PR to convert `{icon}`/`{state}` to `format_on`/`format_off`.

Go for single `format` or multiple `format_*`. Expose everything.

```
# Current (Wrong ; Cannot customize colors everywhere)
format: (default '{host}:{port} {state}')
icon_off: (default 'DOWN')
icon_on: (default 'UP')
```
```
# Ideal (Choice A)
format: (default '{status}')
format_off: (default 'host}:{port} DOWN')
format_on: (default 'host}:{port} UP')
```
```
# Ideal (Choice B)
format: (default '{host}:{port} [\?if=is_open UP|DOWN]'
    OR
format: (default '[\?if=is_open {host}:{port} UP|{host}:{port} DOWN]'
```

**Pro/Con:** Putting everything in the `format`.
* We introduce more placeholders.
* We leave users to figure out formatting and/or controlling.
* We leave users to learn this without a documentation.
* If we change one thing, we hoard all other stuffs in our `format`... and in our config. This includes default options too.
* Cleaner to remove unwanted string in `format` instead of toggling `hide_when_playing` or `hide_when_stopped`. Can be hassle if somebody toggle it back on by adding and/or commenting other `format` below.
* `get_placeholders_list` does not work with `\?if=connected`.
* More potential helpers like `get_control_list` and something to convert `is_connected` boolean to `is_connected` --> `''`,`' '` and add it to the return (auto if possible) ,eg `self.py3.update(is_connected, True)` or `self.py3.update(is_connected, True, data)`. If not `self.py3.update()` due to existing method, then maybe `self.py3.control()`.

**Pro/Con:** Using `format_*`.
* We control if, states, etc in the code.
* Easier for users. Leave less (and no default) options in the config.
* May have to introduce simple options that checks things, simple options that toggles things, etc, but we try to work within `format_*` using placeholders first.
* Set up new standard to have all `format` going with either `'{status}'` or `'{output}'`. Make it easier to add prefix and not having to remember what placeholder it may be. This should work quite well if we don't pass in colors immediately until after `[\?color]` so the statusline color can colorize all module prefix (if any) ie `'GPU[: [\?color]{format_temp}]'` could pass in white `GPU: ` then green `62 F`.

The reason is that mineral fields don't count the harvesters. The command center/ nexus/ hatchery keep track (the number above your base). There should be roughly 2 harvesters per mineral field, which is why the number is 12/16 (12 being your total harvesters and 16 being 2x the number of mineral fields (8)) at the start. If you did base.assigned_harvesters, it would only return the number of workers on the mineral fields, it does not include the geyser worker count.

In case anyone needs help adjusting the size of the matplotlib figure. Since there is an solution, the issue should be closed.

All you need to add is `plt.figure(figsize=(X,Y)`. X and Y being your desired size. Note that the image is being stretched into the figure, so the default width and height makes a blurry image.
```
from wordcloud import WordCloud

word_cloud = WordCloud(width=1000, height=500).generate_from_frequencies(freq_dict)

plt.figure(figsize=(15,10))
plt.imshow(word_cloud, interpolation='bilinear')
plt.axis('off')
```

I agree that having an example for RAW is useful, but the current one is much too complicated and more confusing than helpful.

To a beginner should not be suggested that *writing* a RAW file is generally a good idea, so it would be best if we don't show this in an example. OTOH, a beginner might well be confronted with a RAW file from somebody else, and it would be extremely helpful to see an example how to *read* a RAW file.

```python
import soundfile as sf

data, samplerate = sf.read('myfile.raw', channels=1, samplerate=44100,
                           subtype='FLOAT')
```

To be honest, I have no clue what `endian='FILE'` is supposed to mean for RAW files, do you?
When I tried it, the default seemed to be `endian='LITTLE'`, but I have no idea if this is platform-dependent.
To make this clear, we should probably show an explicit endian-ness like `endian='LITTLE'` ...?

```python
import soundfile as sf

data, samplerate = sf.read('myfile.raw', channels=1, samplerate=44100,
                           subtype='FLOAT', endian='LITTLE')
```

And then we could even mention, that the user should find out by trial and error if `endian='LITTLE'` or `endian='BIG'` is the right thing for the file at hand.

Commit 387f386 adds initial support for manylinux1 wheel packages on Linux.

Uploaded "cefpython3-53.2.dev0-py2.py3-none-manylinux1_x86_64.whl" to PyPI (package for Python 2.7 / 3.4 / 3.5 and 64-bit).

PIP 8.1+ is required to install manylinux1 wheel packages.

Commands that were run to build manylinux1 package after .so files were built for all distributions Python 2.7 / 3.4 / 3.5:

> cd cefpython/src/linux/installer/
> python make-setup.py 53.2
> cd cefpython3*/
> python setup.py bdist_wheel --universal
> cd dist/

The generated file was "cefpython3-53.2-py2.py3-none-any.whl" and it was renamed in two places:

1. Version was changed from "53.2" to "53.2.dev0" to mark it
   as a pre-release so that it installs only with "pip --pre" flag.
   However this doesn't work, it still installs without the --pre flag.
   Looks like it is required to mark it as "dev0" version when creating
   the setup package when running make-setup.py. Adding this postfix
   to version would break examples that make version checks - needs
   further investigation. Still, the "dev0" postfix is visible in file
   that was uploaded to PyPI.
2. Platform tag "-any.whl" was changed to "-manylinux1_x86_64.whl".

I actually had a router begin to fail as I was testing speedtest-cli.  The 'bad' router never reported better than 3.5 down and 1.75 up, it fact it still does not.  I flashed it with the OEM firmware, TomatoUSB, and DD-WRT, all had the same result, horrible throughput.  WIth a correctly functioning router in place, typically the Pis report about 23 down and 2 up, this approximately correct, compared to my PC and server, which report 24-25 down and 2-2.5 up consistently as long as the ISP is stable, which usually it is, knock on wood.  I sample the throughput about every 4 hours, more often it the initial results show degrading from ISP side. Hi,

It took a while to get around to working on support for combined charts in XlsxWriter so I'm happy to report some good progress in time for St. Patrick's day.

There is a beta branch ([combined_chart](https://github.com/jmcnamara/XlsxWriter/tree/combined_chart)) with support for combined charts. You can now create 2 charts and add one to the other to combine them like this:

```python
import xlsxwriter

workbook = xlsxwriter.Workbook('combined_chart1.xlsx')

worksheet = workbook.add_worksheet()

chart1 = workbook.add_chart({'type': 'column'})
chart2 = workbook.add_chart({'type': 'line'})

# Some data to write to the chart.
data = [
    [2, 7, 3, 6, 2],
    [20, 25, 10, 10, 20],
]

worksheet.write_column('A1', data[0])
worksheet.write_column('B1', data[1])

# The primary chart.
chart1.add_series({'values': '=Sheet1!$A$1:$A$5'})

# The secondary chart.
chart2.add_series({'values': '=Sheet1!$B$1:$B$5'})

# Add the secondary chart to the primary chart.
chart1.combine(chart2)

# Insert it in the worksheet.
worksheet.insert_chart('E9', chart1)

workbook.close()
```
![screenshot](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/94267/6676639/5c13248e-cc21-11e4-8299-01d0c2439cb6.png)

Or place the secondary chart on a secondary axis, using the standard syntax:

```python
import xlsxwriter

workbook = xlsxwriter.Workbook('combined_chart2.xlsx')

worksheet = workbook.add_worksheet()

chart1 = workbook.add_chart({'type': 'column'})
chart2 = workbook.add_chart({'type': 'line'})

# Some data to write to the chart.
data = [
    [2, 7, 3, 6, 2],
    [20, 25, 10, 10, 20],
]

worksheet.write_column('A1', data[0])
worksheet.write_column('B1', data[1])

# The primary chart.
chart1.add_series({'values': '=Sheet1!$A$1:$A$5'})

# The secondary chart.
chart2.add_series({'values': '=Sheet1!$B$1:$B$5', 'y2_axis': 1})

# Add the secondary chart to the primary chart.
chart1.combine(chart2)

# Insert it in the worksheet.
worksheet.insert_chart('E9', chart1)

workbook.close()
```
![screenshot](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/94267/6676666/97753d96-cc21-11e4-91e7-6bdbdb74e80b.png)

Both charts can be configured separately before or after they are combined.

The new `chart.combine()` method is the only addition. Everything else is the standard chart interface.

**Terms and Conditions Apply**

This is beta code. It needs a lot more test cases and it is quite possible to break things. Also, scatter charts aren't support yet, but will be shortly.

If you'd like to test it out then please do and let me know if you encounter any issues.

John

"My recommendation is to start apscheduler as a separate process and connect to it via some RPC mechanism to add jobs."

There are five process created by uwsgi each running a flask app along with a apscheduler instance.
So, there are total 5 different scheduler instances.

When we send a request to endpoint, uwsgi passes request to one of the process.
In that endpoint we can add job/remove/modify/pause/resume/get job. 
But apscheduler polls with redis(jobstore) is to check if a job time is due. In the current scenario, in all five processes, apscheduler instance  invoke callback. So, instead of one time job execution, 5 jobs are executed.

Also, you already answered the questions i shared on stackoverflow.

Feature:

If we add the apscheduler job callback in a separate file and that file is executed as a separate apscheduler process.

Then we can have

--> a web-app where multiple scheduler instances are created. Where we can create/delete/add/get operations.

--> a worker process where a single scheduler instance is created. Which is just for executing job when its time come. (callback of any job)

You can always pin to the last one which fully supported 2.x.

A number of scientific projects are also dropping new features, if not also bug fixes, for 2.x next year. See http://www.python3statement.org/#sections40-timeline

This is a great question. I'm not actually sure if there is a good answer
for you right now but I'm hoping that we can figure something out. It might
require making some improvements to ChatterBot itself to make this happen.
Let's see if we can figure something out because I realize this is a real
problem that will only get worse as chat bots build with ChatterBot are
used with larger data sets.

The main problem is that NLP is significantly slower than the set logic
used for querying a database. There are a few approaches that can be used
to make it so that NLP has less to process less data.

*1. Loosening constraints:* One option that could help make this process
faster would be to make the assumption that we do not need to search all of
the data to find the best result. It might be possible that an *optimal
response* could be selected from a subset of the known data.

*2. Queries:* it might be possible to generate a query that eliminates
statements that can't possibly be a response to the input the bot received.
Doing this requires those attributes to be identified but this could help
prevent extraneous statements from being returned in the response from the
database.

An example of this could be that the user asks a question. Let's assume
that all statements in the database now have an `is_question` attribute. We
could eliminate any statement that is not in response to a question.

*3. Preprocessing:* I *very* recently added a preprossessor feature that
makes it possible to do *something* to each statement a chat bot receives
as input. Incoming statements could be tagged with NLP scores and values so
that these could be searched on later. This wouldn't solve the problem for
existing statements but a line could be added somewhere to save the NLP
evaluations if they have not been generated for a statement yet. This makes
it so that NLP evaluations only ever have to be run once for a statement.
This may work well when logic adapters depend on things such as the tagged
parts of speech of a statement.

However, this does not solve a larger portion of the problem which exists
because of the need to make NLP comparisons between statements. For
example, if we want to find the closest known match to a statement, then we
have to compare that statement to *every* other statement. (This is where
I'm currently stuck on how to make this process more efficient.)

My initial reaction to this is that I feel like there should be a better way to handle this. This code is essentially going to duplicate the hostname segment, meaning if there happened to be a change necessary to that segment, I would need to remember to update this code alongside it. Needing to remember this is not a great strategy.

I also think the larger problem you have is just fine-grained control over the way segments are put together. For example, my first idea (not necessarily the best way to do this), would be to give options like `prefix` and `suffix` to every segment. The default would be a single space, but you could then do something like this in your config:

```json
"segments": [
  {"type": "username", "suffix": "@"},
  {"type": "hostname": "prefix": ""}
]
```

I think this deserves more thought, so I am not wanting to rush into incorporating this change.

I disagree. The default in %head should be ~10 or something small. Here are some head and tail functions I wrote for my own projects:

```stata
* heads and tails (aliases for list)
capture program drop head
program head
    syntax [anything] [if] [in], [*]
    if ( regexm(`"`anything'"', "^\s*([0-9]+)") ) {
        local n1 = regexs(1)
        gettoken n2 anything: anything
        cap assert `n1' == `n2'
        if ( _rc ) error 198
        local n = `n1'
    }
    else local n = 10
    if ( `n' < 0 ) error 198
    list `anything' `if' `in' in 1/`n', `options'
end

capture program drop tail
program tail
    syntax [anything] [if] [in], [*]
    if ( regexm(`"`anything'"', "^\s*([0-9]+)") ) {
        local n1 = regexs(1)
        gettoken n2 anything: anything
        cap assert `n1' == `n2'
        if ( _rc ) error 198
        local n = `n1'
    }
    else local n = 10
    if ( `n' < 0 ) error 198
    list `anything' `if' `in' in -`n'/l, `options'
end
```

Re: Pros

- No, you don't always get a dot. You'd need to add some foo.
- Yes, it does stop on error.
- It has the parent locals available, modifies `set` and locals as expected.

Cons:
- I wrote a wrapper that sends stata(`"cmd"') if in mata. If you head the file with `mata` and end with `end' you'd be (mostly) fine.
- Mata execution does not stop on error with include OR with do.
- It's almost surely slower, and the lag for simple one-liners (pwd, cd, desc, etc.) might be annoying.

If there's a long pause after "Waiting for session", that means there are no more available connections in the connection pool: https://github.com/ecederstrand/exchangelib/blob/9ef6d58b44c33bf5cf5164a5b96cf118ec57cdc1/exchangelib/protocol.py#L92

If you're calling exchangelib methods in multiple threads, then you can raise the session poolsize: `Protocol.SESSION_POOLSIZE = 10`. The `10` is just an example; it should be lower than whatever concurrent connection policy the Exchange server has set on your account.

That looks good. You can simplify things further by changing it into this:

        reversion.default_revision_manager.save_revision((p,))        
        p.delete()

        deleted_list = reversion.get_deleted(Parent1)
        self.assertNotEqual(0, len(deleted_list))

        deleted_version = deleted_list[0]
        deleted_version.revision.revert()

Wrapping the deletion in a revision isn't strictly necessary. It just logs the deletion in the system for those concerned about statistics.

Using default_revision_manager.save_revision isn't strictly part of the official API, but it works. It's a nice shortcut to create revisions on the fly when you don't want to go through the hassle of re-saving the model.

I tend to agree with you:

- The use of `smtps` scheme is confusing and should be discouraged or even deprecated in a new release. It was a bad idea.

- We should add new proposed submit:// scheme so it can be used optionally.

But I am worry about breaking existing deployments.

So, before changing the current behavior I think we need to: 

1. Improve documentation including the preferred way of configuring popular email services as [Gmail](https://stackoverflow.com/a/31333304) or [Sendgrid](https://sendgrid.com/docs/Integrate/Frameworks/django.html) using port 567 and STARTTLS.

```
EMAIL_URL=smtp://user@gmail.com:s3cr3t@smtp.google.com:587?tls=true
```

2. Add deprecation notice and warnings announcing `smtps` is not recommended and that it will be removed in the future. We need to pay special attention to the use of `smtps:` without tls nor ssl options explicitly because these are the cases that will be broken by the new release.

Then, we will be ready to include these proposed changes.

Firstly, It is fully testing your apps with full unit tests. The app is running the same way as you start your service during unit tests.

It's just the way organize your code/testing code.

Your example is pretty simple, think about that if your service is interacting with `Mongo` or any db. In some unit tests, you're testing db behaviors or some complicated logic. It doesn't make sense to unit test against your production env if importing/using the global app. This is pretty normal i think.

This piece of code i post is logically identical to your code. and it's much more flexible and testable.

```python
from .app import create_app

@pytest.yield_fixture
def app():
    app = create_app(test_config, **params)
    yield app

@pytest.fixture
def test_cli(loop, app, test_client):

Conceptually I support the idea of providing consumers of this code with a way to do custom stuff to the names it while still retaining the useful parts of the parser. I also think it's probably worthwhile for this code to provide a way to remove a set of symbols from names when you just want to remove some junk in them.

Your code doesn't actually prevent the symbols from being added to the internal lists, but instead just prevents them from displaying when using the property methods. So I think that the symbols would still appear with `list(hn)`, for example. Seems cleaner to keep them out of the lists in the first place.

There is a `post_process()` method that happens at the end of the name parsing. Would implementing support for something like the following be a better solution for a way to do custom stuff to the names? Currently you'd have to subclass and override `post_process()` to do the same thing. 

```python
import re
def remove_symbols(instance):
    for attr in instance:
        setattr(instance, attr, re.sub('@!&+*', '', getattr(instace, attr)))
  
name = HumanName("Mick '@mickjagger' Jagger", postprocessing_callback=remove_symbols)
```

Since you're dealing with a HumanName instance here, it requires you to understand a bit about what that is in order to loop through it and do stuff with the names. Maybe it would be better to do it in a `pre_process()`. Since we're still dealing with a single string at that point, you wouldn't need to know as much about the internals of how the parser works. 

```python
def remove_symbols(string):
    return ''.join([symbol for symbol in string if symbol not in '@!&+*'])
  
name = HumanName("Mick '@mickjagger' Jagger", preprocessing_callback=remove_symbols)
```

As for removing symbols, I would probably add a `self.remove_symbols = ""`, set it in `__init__()` and then just always remove any characters included in that string. Do it in the `post_process()` so that it doesn't break stuff like "Jack & Jill".

I agree it is hard to have good intuition on the nugget values, however you may use this to guide you a bit:
>  In the special case of the squared exponential correlation function, the nugget mathematically represents the variance of the input values.

Increasing its value may slow down the optimization, but it may also help by giving the GP more flexibility. In principle the maximum will always be found, even if it takes forever, but in practice you have to make a judgment call when combining nugget with the parameters of the acquisition function as well.

In the case of a step function I believe you should avoid using the squared exponential kernel and, instead, go for something like the absolute exponential. Square exp. heavily favours smooth functions and can struggle with steps, kinks, and similarly non-differentiable points.

> Amazon S3 provides read-after-write consistency for PUTS of new objects in your S3 bucket in all regions with one caveat. > Updates to a single key are atomic. For example, if you PUT to an existing key, a subsequent read might return the old data or the updated data, but it will never write corrupted or partial data.

Adding my own comment here since the owner doesn't appear excited to answer. I find it's best to just drop that constraint altogether. You'll have plenty of instances in which the postal_code will differ but the state_code and name in the address_locality table will remain the same. You can fix this with the following steps:

`SHOW CREATE TABLE address_locality;`

find the index that binds "name" and "state_id", and drop the constraint with that name. In my case, address_locality_name_5eb72081b80e9d3d_uniq.

`ALTER TABLE address_locality DROP INDEX address_locality_name_5eb72081b80e9d3d_uniq`

Here is an example of how I use it in real world. This packs and unpacks all arguments for an event handler with ``msgpack`` transparently.

```
import msgpack

class CodingMiddleware:
  def ignore_event(self, event, namespace):
    return event in ("connect", "disconnect")

  def before_event(self, event, namespace, args):
    assert len(args) == 2  # sid and packed args
    arg = None
    assert isinstance(args[-1], bytes)
    unpacked_args = msgpack.loads(args[-1], encoding="utf-8")
    assert isinstance(unpacked_args, list)
    args.pop()
    args.extend(unpacked_args)

  def after_event(self, event, namespace, ret):
    packed_ret = msgpack.dumps(ret, use_bin_type=True)
    ret.clear()
    ret.append(packed_ret)
```

I simply add it to all event handlers of all namespaces:
```
sio = socketio.Server()
sio.middlewares.append(CodingMiddleware())
```

> I'm sorry, can you write real example, how I can get expected behavior from item 4 of my first post?

First, there are two kinds of code blocks: standard Markdown indented code blocks, and then fenced blocks.

````
    ```
    some code
    ```
````

Which gives you 

    ```
    some code
    ```

Notice the whole fences are indented, so they are counted as code.

Now you have fenced:

````
```
some code
```
````

And you get

```
some code
```

Notice the fences this time are counted as tokens for highlighting.  This is why I am saying you are indenting your code to much in your example.  But when you deindent you don't get a fence block because **only** superfences will provide highlighting on a fence nested under a list.

So here is your example with fixed indentation.

```
1. First
1. Second

    ```python
    my Python code
    ```

    Text after second

1. Third
```

Here is a config file to send parameters to the extensions.  You could just as easily replace `pymdownx.highlight` with `markdown.extensions.codehilite`, but in this example, we will use `highlight`.

```js
{
    "pymdownx.highlight": {
        "use_pygments": false
    }
}
```

Command line:

```
python -m markdown -x pymdownx.superfences -x pymdownx.highlight -c extension.json test.md
```

Output:

```html

    

  1. First
    2. 

  2. 

    Second
    

    my Python code
    



    Text after second
    

    3. 

  3. 

    Third
    

    4. 


```

That's how you get no Pygments and nested highlighting.  I am aware of the excessive whitespace after the code block.  It's harmless, but it will be taken care of in a future version.

Hey @fiusuki,
So the way `django-chunked-upload` works is it creates a file called `<upload_id>.part`, and it uses it to append the chunks you send on each request.
Once you finish uploading all the chunks and send the `/complete` request, the `on_completion`method will be called, and the uploaded file will be passed as a parameter.
In this method is where you'd implement whatever logic you want to do with that file. Could be store it somewhere in your media folder, associate it to a model's instance, open the file and analyze/parse it, etc. That's entirely up to you.
In the demo, [the `on_completion` method is not doing anything](https://github.com/juliomalegria/django-chunked-upload-demo/blob/master/demo/views.py#L30), that's why you only see the `.part` file.
I hope that answers your question :)

I think the behavior should be this:
- .validate_and_* should NOT ignore writable=False fields. That variable is a pass-through value that should only be used by forms
- it should ONLY validate fields that are passed as input
- .validate_and_insert should nevertheless loop and insert default values also for fields not passed as input which may have a default value
- .validate_and_update should not loop over fields not passed as input unless they have an update value.
This is what was originally intended.

```
import mxnet as mx
from mxnet import gluon, autograd

vocab_size = 10
num_embed = 100
num_hidden = 1024
layout = "TNC"


class Encoder(gluon.Block):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        with self.name_scope():
            self.embedding = mx.gluon.nn.Embedding(vocab_size, num_embed)
            self.rnn = mx.gluon.rnn.GRU(hidden_size=num_hidden, layout=layout)

    def forward(self, inputs, hidden):
        emb = self.embedding(inputs)
        output, hidden = self.rnn(emb, hidden)
        return output, hidden


class Decoder(gluon.Block):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        with self.name_scope():
            self.embedding = mx.gluon.nn.Embedding(vocab_size, num_embed)
            self.rnn = mx.gluon.rnn.GRU(hidden_size=num_hidden, layout=layout)
            self.projection = mx.gluon.nn.Dense(units=vocab_size)

    def forward(self, inputs, hidden):
        emb = self.embedding(inputs)
        output, hidden = self.rnn(emb, hidden)
        projection = self.projection(output.reshape((-1, num_hidden)))
        projection = projection.reshape((-1, inputs.shape[1], vocab_size))
        return projection, hidden


encoder = Encoder()
decoder = Decoder()
loss = gluon.loss.SoftmaxCrossEntropyLoss(batch_axis=1)

encoder.collect_params().initialize()
decoder.collect_params().initialize()

encoder.hybridize()
decoder.hybridize()

data = mx.nd.zeros((10, 8), mx.cpu())
begin_state = encoder.rnn.begin_state(
    func=mx.nd.zeros, batch_size=8, ctx=mx.cpu())
with autograd.record():
    output, hidden = encoder(data, begin_state)
    projection, _ = decoder(data, hidden)
    l = loss(projection, data)
l.backward()

```

#Freeze the current env to the watchman config. This is a hack to get around
# https://github.com/facebook/watchman/issues/68

PLIST=~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.github.facebook.watchman.plist

# "</dict>"
echo "<key>EnvironmentVariables</key><dict>"$ENVIRONMENT"</dict></dict>" >> $PLIST

# Replace the last line

# Reload the watchman config

Found the issue: another coroutine was hogging the loop exactly after the burst in messages. Not very easy to find , I might add, as the warning message is misleading. Instead of mentioning the culprit coroutine, the warning mentions the one returning from the long wait.

I think it's a bug in the way the ```debug=True``` mode warnings interacts with fast spinning loops and long-blocking calls. Need to dissect this further I guess as it's still not clear if it's ZMQLoop related or pure asyncio.

For posterity:
Make sure your ```while condition_is_true:``` loops are not spinning very often. Ideally add test cases for all loop internals using the ```resource``` module.

```
class CPUBoundAudit(object):
    def __enter__(self):
        self.ru = resource.getrusage(resource.RUSAGE_SELF)[0]
        self.tm = time.monotonic()

    def __exit__(self, exc_type, value, tb):
        self.ru = resource.getrusage(resource.RUSAGE_SELF)[0] - self.ru
        self.tm = time.monotonic() - self.tm
        usage = self.ru / self.tm if self.tm > 0 else 0
        assert usage < 0.25 # Use less than 25% CPU
```

I guess the point of this PR is to offer the option to compose multiple (often independent) validators, particularly in conjunction with #336, which I think is a good thing. My point is that the natural composition method is different for validation and coercion: Validators enforce constraints that are effectively composed via a logical `and`, while coercion are chained functions. Therefore I would like to suggest to separate these two concepts well enough in any new API. The nice thing about order free composition of validation allows orthogonal validation concepts to co-exist without knowing of each other. This has many merits for introspection/metaprogramming. Take for example an automatic GUI generation framework that translates traits to a UI: Knowing about the constraints allows it to reflect these constraints to the user. If there are many orthogonal constraints, it could make use of all known ones and ignore the rest. Take an automatic remote procedure call library that is able to expose traits on the proxy object: Knowing the constraints allows it to optimise the protocol. I have written and used such a library for the original traits, as well as a few other meta-programming tricks. Several times I had to work around poor separation of concepts. Traitlets has proven to value good API design (deprecating magic method names in favour of explicit decorators), why not continue that here?

> @gamebusterz <https://github.com/gamebusterz> There are some usecases
> where priority is useful:
>
> You have a workflow of tasks containing different families of tasks:
>
>    - A type of Task used to compute data used in your webapp (Example:
>    playlist prefered by users)
>    - A type of Task to report user actions on your webapp (Example: data
>    used to train machine learning models)
>    - A type of Task to compute some informations about your business
>
> The three family of tasks are unrelated (directly and indirectly).
>
> But the first task family has higher priority (because it's used directly
> in your webapp by real users).
>
> You workflow looks like:
>
> class MyBusinessWorkflow(luigi.Task):
>
>     requires(self):
>          yield ComputeUsersActions() # priority 60
>          yield ComputeUsersPlaylists() # priority 100
>          yield ComputeBusinessReport() # priority 20
>
> It means luigi will execute firstly the highest priority. But if you have
> multiple workers, luigi will not wait highest priority task is done.
>
> Priority is just used to organise unrelated tasks execution, not to give a
> precise order.

So I think you could actually solve this with just a presigned `GetObject`. If the content type is the only the thing you need from the `HeadObject`, you only need to generate a presigned url for the `GetObject` because the content type comes back on the `GET` request. You can do that by do the following:
```python
>>> import boto3
>>> c = boto3.client('s3')
>>> c.generate_presigned_url('get_object', Params={'Bucket':'mybucketfoo', 'Key':'foo'})
```
And if you use `httpie` on it, you can see the content type come back in the response:
```
$ http 'https://mybucketfoo.s3.amazonaws.com/foo?AWSAccessKeyId=...'
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Accept-Ranges: bytes
Content-Length: 8
Content-Type: binary/octet-stream
Date: Wed, 15 Nov 2017 16:06:31 GMT
ETag: "655aa04125535c42e74725770dd7f2db"

It started when I tried reducing a list of filter expressions like so:
```python
from functools import reduce
from boto3.dynamodb.conditions import Attr

def query_generator(query_string):
    filter_expressions = list()
    if '<=' in query_string:
        key, value = query_string.split('<=')
        filter_expressions.append(Attr(key).lte(value))
    elif '>=' in query_string:
        key, value = query_string.split('>=')
        filter_expressions.append(Attr(key).gte(value))
    else: return None
    return reduce(lambda x, y: x & y, filter_expressions)
```

  warnings.warn(msg)

@michamos, what are we going to do with `misc`, `eprint`, and `supplemental-material`?

_From @michamos on April 24, 2017 8:41_

`eprint` -> `article` as we don't distinguish between published/unpublished but only peer reviewed/non-peer reviewed. `misc` seems to indicate a reference that APS didn't know how to interpret (also footnotes sometimes, as they are mixed with the references in Phys.Rev.) and `supplemental-material` is for extra information to the paper (appendices that didn't make the length cut). So you can leave them out, as they don't translate to anything in our data model.

_From @annetteholtkamp on July 21, 2016 12:1_

773__0 contains the recid of the book. Usually the 773 field for a book chapter contains just
773__0 and 773__c (page range).
But it may also be 773__i (for ISBN) and 773__c.

One could also imagine using the DOI of the book to refer to it. In principle it would be great if many identifiers are allowed to save cataloguers the work of looking up the recid.

We also have links via report number. E.g..
773__r may refer to a CERN yellow report

We do not support django-tabbed-admin, and as far as I can tell it is not widely used enough for us to officially support it. That said, if you want to have any hope of getting the two to work together, you’ll want to change this line in your admin.py above:

```python
class TableOfContentsAdmin(TabbedModelAdmin, nested_admin.NestedModelAdmin): 
    # ...
```

to

```python
class TableOfContentsAdmin(nested_admin.NestedModelAdminMixin, TabbedModelAdmin): 
    # ...
```

If the problem persists after making that change, I think it would make more sense to open an issue on the [django-tabbed-admin](https://github.com/omji/django-tabbed-admin) project.

What I think is happening is that the empty “extra” forms are not being validated, which is a Django feature. If you add new forms, those might have the `order` field set on them, so Django would not consider them empty, and thus it performs the validation checks on those inlines. I suspect that if you were to fill in one field on those inlines (but not all of the required fields) then the validation errors would show up consistently on all inlines.

Is there a workaround, or is flit permanently incompatible with pipenv?

Note that the *current* version of flit specifies requires `Requires-Python: >=3`. This has been true of, all versions of flit as of flit v0.1, released 2016-10-16. Yet the presence of the *previous* versions of flit in pypi prevents pipenv (but not pip) from installing *recent* versions of flit. `flit = "*"`, `flit = "1.0"`, and `"flit = "≧1.0"`, in `[packages]` or `[dev-packages]`, all cause `pipenv install` to fail.

Also note that version specifiers of the form `Requires-Python: 3` were valid from 2005 -04-28([PEP 345](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0345/)) through 2009-0604 ([PEP 386](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0386/)). The [“Requires Python” section of PEP 345](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0345/#requires-python) gives `Requires-Python: 2.5` as an example. Although the offending flit releases were not written within this window, it's likely that other packages had releases during that time, or incorrectly used that version description format even after PEP 386 changed it. If I understand this issue correctly, pipenv will fail to install those packages too.

`.env` files are loaded by pipenv only for the purpose of and in the context of running your code inside your virtualenv (e.g. running an application in a virtualenv). `pipenv install -e .` is the same as `python setup.py develop`.  The resulting pipfile and lockfile will never be usable without the requisite `setup.py`.  Pipfiles are not meant to be used for user installation of a package, they are a development tool.

Lockfiles are designed to resolve dependency graphs.  Installing a non-editable zipfile isn't a good way to handle that, and if you install `-e .` typically you would expect the environment to be updated anytime you modify the package -- that's the whole point of the workflow.

This isn't really how python packaging works, basically, so I don't see this workflow as something we really want to adopt.  Thanks for the well constructed suggestion, and I can see where you're coming from.  It's just not a practice we want to encourage.

I fixed tests for the Vietnamese (btw weekdays are processed by dateutil parser, not by freshness. Think about it as freshness only parse forms like "... ago" and you can not say "Thursday ago").
Although there are couple of cases i did not fix and i think it is best to go without such support:
* "19 Jan 2010 lúc 2:19pm", this date is mixing two languages, so it is not a valid date to consider. You can simply fix it with `re.sub(ur"\blúc\b", "", date_string")` in spider and it would be valid english dates/
* "Thg 11 25", while in this particular case it is obvious that it is November, it is not quite clear in the string like "1 năm 1 tháng 1 tuần". It is most likely "1 year 1 month 1 day" but could also be "1 year 1 january day". I think it is better for now to substitute such dates with "Tháng mười một" in spider.

I foresee a few ways to solve this issue...

`scrapyd-deploy` could support some kind of `oauth2_bearer_token` key in the `scrapy.cfg` file. This could add the HTTP `Authorization` header for us automatically.

```
[deploy]
url = https://scrapyd.example.com/
project = example
oauth2_bearer_token = 1/AbC123
```

A more generic solution might be to simply allow users to specify additional HTTP headers manually via command-line arguments. This would allow us to use OAuth2 authentication but would also allow other use cases which require custom headers.

```
scrapyd-deploy -h "Authorization: Bearer 1/AbC123", "Another-Header: foo"
```

Hey @brandur-stripe, thanks for the feedback :)

To clarify the current design:

- the only difference that **must** be addressed between API requests to `api.stripe.com` and to `connect.stripe.com` is error handling, as the error objects that are returned have different formats. `connect.stripe.com`'s errors are hashes with two attributes: `error` containing an error code and (optionally) `error_description` containing a human-readable description of the error.

  Everything else (authentication, parameter encoding, etc.) is compatible. The Connect docs say that the API key should be passed in a `client_secret` POST parameter [here](https://stripe.com/docs/connect/reference#post-token), but that's just because that's how it is in the OAuth spec. Passing the API key in the `Authorization: Bearer ...` header works just fine.

- there is no new abstract class for `APIRequestor` -- I just added a new `OAuthRequestor` class that directly derives from `APIRequestor` and overrides the `handle_api_error` and `interpret_response` methods to properly handle errors. (Admittedly, maybe I _should_ have created a new `AbstractRequestor` class and make both `APIRequestor` and `OAuthRequestor` derive from it.)

- for tests, I introduced two abstract classes: `AbstractStripeApiTestCase` and `AbstractRequestorRequestTests` and made both the existing `StripeApiTestCase` / `APIRequestorRequestTests` and the new `StripeOAuthTestCase` / `OAuthRequestorRequestTests` derive from them. I did it this way because test classes are instantiated directly by `unittest` and using class attributes was the only way I could think of to parameterize them with the appropriate `*Requestor` classes.

I don't necessarily have anything against composition over inheritance, but I'm having a hard time wrapping my head around how it would apply here. If you can give me a quick example of what you had in mind, I'll be happy to comment on it and/or make some changes to the code :)

Sorry for being away for long. So let's finalize this filter.
Theoretically, it can accept any input, even complex, and it could emit any type.
However, the high pass filter in it requires floats for meaningful precision,  
so internally, I use floats.

What about this interpretation:
- I use floats internally
- when (u)integers are emitted, they will fit into the dynamic range, regardless of the
  preserve_range keyword. If clipping is required, so be it.
- when the input is float, and preserve range is False, then the output is float, without clipping, or without any constraints on the input domain. (Could be outside of [-1,1]).
- when the input is float, and the preserve_range is True:
   - I require input from[0,1], and the output will be [0,1], using clipping if necessary.
   - if the input is not from [0,1], then the code will emit warning, clip the input for [0,1], and 
then process it as it is. (Or it could stop with assert, but I do not like this.)
- color channel: they will be processed individually (RGB or similar primary based model),
  but the docstring gets a comment about using HSV/Lab/etc color spaces where
  only the lightness/brightness/luminance/etc. channel should be processed. 
  (But I will not implement any implicit conversion.)

Is this interpretation acceptable, or do you have any other recommendation?
If you agree with these, I will send a fixed commit soon. (If you don't, then please specify your interpretation.)

@jni But are we willing to sacrifice readability?  Reading code will be like reading a book without chapter names. Grouping functions into modules makes the programmer want to explore the module he is using. A person reading  `skimage.all.BRIEF` will have to put in more effort to find out about other features like DAISY or CENSURE.

I think reading takes more importance over writing.  That's also one of the philosopies behind Python. I maybe wrong,  but I think attracting new users is important. And a newbie spends a lot more time reading code, unlike everyone on this thread.

@stefanv yes, `preserve_range`, so @JDWarner if you contrast-adjust a uint8 image, you convert to float, by definition it gets cast to [0, 1] unless `preserve_range` is set to `True`. The decorator can be aware of this keyword.

At any rate, I'm not sure it can catch *all* use cases. ie maybe a decorator is not the right approach compared to a function-specific fix.

> Do we really want any generic Invenio production site to be slowed down by deprecation alerts?

> [...]

> we don't have the resources to fix all them (not even Invenio has existing replacement for all the technologies we are deprecating(!))

Here are my 2 cents on this topic.

As we are splitting Invenio into many packages, and as Invenio v2.1+ is becoming literally a framework of independent components, it will often happen that some Invenio v1.x facility does not have its counterpart in v2.1 and above yet.  Installations needing these features can run Invenio v2.0 which contains fully all the legacy modules.  Installations not needing these features can progressively upgrade to v2.1, v2.2, v2.3, etc, as we shall be cleaning the Invenio core and progressively releasing modules as separate packages.  This may bring many, many deprecation warnings along the way in the coming months, before v3.0 will be finally out.

From the point of view of Invenio being more and more a Pythonic library, instead of a monolithic ILS application, it seems natural to emit deprecation warnings by default, and relying on people switching them out on their production system.  After all, we don't recommend to run Invenio v2.x in production yet; and people testing it out always start on their developer instances anyway.  So, if we provide clear documentation on how to run Invenio in production -- which necessarily passes by creating an overlay -- it seems enough to me to simply document in big fat letters how people can switch various warnings off on their production systems, if they want to.  But, since Invenio v2.x is marketed as a developer version, it seems consistent to me to emit as much warnings as possible, so that services are informed early and can catch and fix any troubles as soon as possible. When the time comes that we shall start recommending Invenio v2.N for production one day, we could perhaps consider switching the default the other way...  The question is, has the time come already to flip the switch that way?  Or not yet?

P.S. It may be interesting to note that Python itself was showing deprecation warnings by default in 2.6 and earlier versions, but as of 2.7 it is not showing them anymore, unless people explicitly ask for them via CLI switch. https://docs.python.org/2/using/cmdline.html#cmdoption-W

This is a program for doing bayesian uncertainty modeling with Markov Chain Monte Carlo and/or bayesian maximum likelihood estimation using standard optimization algorithms (i.e., non-linear regression).  Problem definitions are set up to be familiar to those in the physical sciences, where measurements have uncertainty.

Bumps is probably not useful for the usual problems in ML, which tend to have too large a parameter space for the algorithms in bumps to search effectively.

@jpadilla I think, the signal should be put out of both the authentication classes and serializer. The serailizer can be used at various occasions and emitting user_logged_in signals during all those cases may not be always desired. Maybe it should be left to the developers to decide when to emit this signals. 

But if we had to emit a signal in the first place, ObtainJSONWebToken view maybe the right place. Comparing to any login view we generally use in Django, where the given credentials are verified and the user is logged in --> session created for further use + signals sent, the ObtainJSONWebToken can be a place where we send the signal: the credentials are verified and the user is logged in (not really)  --> token created for further use + signals sent. Still, I think leaving the user_logged_in signal usage in case of django-rest-jwt to the developers is the best.
