I don't think adding a flag is appropriate here. If Fire provides a choice (sys.exit vs return) then it should provide that choice to the client code calling Fire, not to the end user. The end user won't have context as to whether it makes sense for the program to keep running after the call to Fire or not.

When Fire hits a usage error the options are:
1. Display the error and then exit w/ a status code.
2. Display the error and return the error (wrapped in some object) to the client.
3. Display the error and raise the error to the client.

The downside of 1 is that it provides the client no way of handling the error.
The downside of 2 is that the default use case (just calling fire.Fire) doesn't get proper status codes.
The downside of 3 is that the default use case (just calling fire.Fire) will show a Python trace in the case of a usage error, which isn't the user experience we want.

I lean toward option 2 because it makes getting proper status codes a simple 1-liner for people who want it (Something like `result = fire.Fire(); result.ExitIfError()`; exact syntax tbd.) and also allows the client the flexibility to handle the result of fire (error or not) however they chose. Option 1, while it gets status codes right, provides no way for people to handle usage errors themselves.

thanks for bringing this up, i've also frequently been annoyed by this, but let me explain why it is this way:

back when we discovered that narrow python builds don't really support unicode chars in the supplementary planes we found this highly annoying:
```python
﻿﻿﻿﻿﻿In [1]: u'\U0010ffff'
Out[1]: u'\U0010ffff'

In [2]: print u'\U0010ffff'
﻿
In [3]: len(u'\U0010ffff')
Out[3]: 2

In [4]: u'\U0010ffff'.encode('utf-8')
Out[4]: '\xf4\x8f\xbf\xbf'

In [5]: '\xf4\x8f\xbf\xbf'.decode('utf-8')
Out[5]: u'\U0010ffff'

In [6]: len('\xf4\x8f\xbf\xbf'.decode('utf-8'))
Out[6]: 2

In [7]: u'\U0010ffff'[0:1]
Out[7]: u'\udbff'

In [8]: u'\U0010ffff'[1:2]
Out[8]: u'\udfff'

In [9]: u'\U0010ffff'[0:1].encode('utf-8')
Out[9]: '\xed\xaf\xbf'

In [10]: u'\U0010ffff'[1:2].encode('utf-8')
Out[10]: '\xed\xbf\xbf'
```

So while your fix works (and is probably better than just crashing out in case you encounter a char > 0xFFFF) it is dangerous as soon as you do something with the strings like slicing, regexps based on string length, ...

Back then the decision was: UGH, use a wide python build.

Given that narrow python builds are the default on mac os x and binary packages like scipy aren't well compatible with wide python builds on these platforms, i'd now opt for: use a wide python build, but if you use a narrow build: warning to the user and best effort solution instead of crashing.

I think this is correct behavior.

There are two different contracts:

1. Patroni should first set a node name into the synchronous_standby_names and only after that write this info into DCS. If Patroni decides (or get a command) that node should not be synchronous, it first removes this info from DCS and only after that from synchronous_standby_names. The order is very important. Otherwise we don't know which node was synchronous when master has died. 

2. Contract with synchronous_mode_strict is slightly different. Master should not perform writes if there is no synchronous standby available (transaction must be persisted by two nodes). Writes are not possible if there are no standbys available. This is very bad for the application. Therefore Patroni is doing the best it can, puts `*` into synchronous_standby_names. As a result, barman (or even the node with nosync flag) can temporarily become a sync standby until somebody better candidate appears in the cluster. After that Patroni will again change synchronous_standby_names and put the name of the good node into it.

The major difference between sync strict and non strict is that in non strict mode master will remove synchronous_standby_names from the config. That basically disables sync replication.

En effet, le script fait appel à [`ssconvert`](https://help.gnome.org/users/gnumeric/stable/sect-files-ssconvert.html.en) (fourni par le logiciel [Gnumeric](http://www.gnumeric.org/)) afin de convertir les fichiers XLSX en XLS, puis utilise le paquet Python [`xlrd`](http://xlrd.readthedocs.io) pour extraire les données du XLS.

Nous n'avons pas encore compris pourquoi (versions différentes ?) mais sous macOS les fichiers XLS écrits par `ssconvert` ne sont pas lisibles par `xlrd`. En revanche les fichiers écrits depuis Debian puis copiés sous macOS sont bien lisibles par `xlrd`. 

Quoi qu'il en soit une piste alternative consisterait à utiliser [`openpyxl`](http://openpyxl.readthedocs.io) pour extraire les données directement depuis les fichiers XLSX, sans passer par `ssconvert`.
Cette piste nécessite d'adapter le script [`xls_to_yaml_raw.py`](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-france/blob/17aaadd73d4a1f9878a05b15810843d677dd14ef/openfisca_france/scripts/parameters/baremes_ipp/xls_to_yaml_raw.py) pour utiliser l'API d'`openpyxl`, et après tentative j'en conclus que ce ne sera pas possible rapidement d'ici jeudi.

I think we're in agreement about `Table` :) The way I see it `Table` would be just an abstract representation of a table in CARTO. Like you said, it'd just have metadata that would allow other operations to act on it and perform database operations in CARTO's cloud.

For me `Table` is just a convenience for labeling a table in the database instead of having to do `SELECT * FROM table` -- and allows an easy interface for users to create nodes from all rows and columns of a table.

When I say `Table` should inherit from query, I mean that `Table` is just `SELECT * FROM table`, so any operations that you perform on `Table` should be perform-able on `Query` too. Just like Builder analysis, the node doesn't have to be the original table query, but can be an arbitrary query.

Happy to have this discussion :)

I ended up writing a simpler pagination function:
```python
def paginate(function, args=None, kwargs=None, maxResults=1000):
    if args is None: args = []
    if kwargs is None: kwargs = {}
    results = []
    kwargs['startAt'] = 0
    kwargs['maxResults'] = maxResults
    while True:
        try:
            page = function(*args, **kwargs)
        except jira.JIRAError as je:
            print(je)
            page = []
        if not page:
            break
        results.extend(page)
        kwargs['startAt'] += len(page)
    return results
```
Call it like: `results = paginate(jira_obj.search_issues, "project = MYPROJ")`

Main use for me was to get the "fetch all" of maxResults=None, but more than 50 a request.

1.  Here's an example app:

```python
@app.route('/')
def broken():
    app.current_request.raw_body
    return app.current_request.to_dict()
```

This app currently crashes.  If you omit the line `app.current_request.raw_body` then the app will not crash.  The reason for this is because in the chalice handler code, we construct the dict that lambda needs, so we have to serialize the body to JSON (by default in line 315):

https://github.com/aws/chalice/blob/f787f3e53cc346dbfa42c49331ee147a09451365/chalice/app.py#L312-L323

When you access `app.current_request.raw_body`, this value is also computed lazily in a property, so accessing `.raw_body` will internally cache this computed value as `self._raw_body`, and `self._raw_body` is of type `Optional[bytes]`:

https://github.com/aws/chalice/blob/f787f3e53cc346dbfa42c49331ee147a09451365/chalice/app.py#L278-L287

However if you don't access the `.raw_body` property, then `self._raw_body` is `None`, which serializes to `null` in JSON without issues.


2.  Yes: http://chalice.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#Request.to_dict   .  We have some limited tests but nothing that checks all the different ways a request object can be constructed.  I think I may have found a legitimate use for hypothesis in chalice.

Ah yes, I forgot that not all tables have `id` column. In that case `SELECT COUNT(1)` would be best.

As far as I know, some databases recognize what the user really wants and only returns the rown coutn when `SELECT COUNT(*)` is performed. But even if they don't, it will still be more efficient than `len(self.all())` as the counting is done by the DB, as opposed to having all the rows transferred (possibly over the network) to Python, having the content evaluated and then counted.

I think `SELECT COUNT(1)` is a safer choice.

Several RFCs explicitly point out the security issue created by canonicalizing hostnames in GSSAPI using DNS.

 For example, RFC4462 (for SSH GSS-API methods) says this:

>  Because the GSS-API mechanism uses the targ_name to authenticate the
>    server's identity, it is important that it be determined in a secure
>    fashion.  One common way to do this is to construct the targ_name
>    from the hostname as typed by the user; unfortunately, because some
>    GSS-API mechanisms do not canonicalize hostnames, it is likely that
>    this technique will fail if the user has not typed a fully-qualified,
>    canonical hostname.  Thus, implementers may wish to use other
>    methods, but should take care to ensure they are secure.  For
>    example, one should not rely on an unprotected DNS record to map a
>    host alias to the primary name of a server, or an IP address to a
>    hostname, since an attacker can modify the mapping and impersonate
>    the server.
> 
>    Implementations of mechanisms conforming to this document MUST NOT
>    use the results of insecure DNS queries to construct the targ_name.
>    Clients MAY make use of a mapping provided by local configuration or
>    use other secure means to determine the targ_name to be used.  If a
>    client system is unable to securely determine which targ_name to use,
>    then it SHOULD NOT use this mechanism.

RFC4752 (for the Kerberos V5 (GSSAPI) SASL mechanism) has this to say:

> Security issues are discussed throughout this memo.
> 
>    When constructing the input_name_string, the client SHOULD NOT
>    canonicalize the server's fully qualified domain name using an
>    insecure or untrusted directory service.

It's not explicitly forbidden with GSSAPI itself, but since tools are in place for a properly administered kerberos site to decide to canonicalize or not based on a site wide policy, we should not make a decision which counter-acts that global policy by default.

ENV VARS: In the [pull request build][1] the usual players changed. In the ["push" build][2], there are some differences:

```
# These ones seem to change with each PR / push
TRAVIS_SECURE_ENV_VARS: true               # vs. false
TRAVIS_BRANCH: dont-merge-2277-sleuthing   # vs. master
TRAVIS_EVENT_TYPE: push                    # vs. pull_request
TRAVIS_PULL_REQUEST: false                 # vs. 2309
```

Also `TRAVIS_COMMIT` is the actual commit at the tip of the branch (i.e. the one that was pushed, c274f02461c82627cc10a71185df46e6091f97a8) and `TRAVIS_COMMIT_RANGE` covers a range I can't quite parse. The beginning commit (7147a65354d1905b225dc4ab96487cfa17746bed) isn't in the branch history while the 2nd (c274f02461c82627cc10a71185df46e6091f97a8) is just the final commit in that branch. The mystery commit seems to have the same parent and to be the same commit, so I'm not sure what the difference is (maybe worth digging into the [GitHub hooks API][3]):

```
$ git log -1 7147a65354d1905b225dc4ab96487cfa17746bed^ --pretty=%H
58ef62114a8f38295be5fa93311593c8eeb66683
$ git log -1 c274f02461c82627cc10a71185df46e6091f97a8^ --pretty=%H
58ef62114a8f38295be5fa93311593c8eeb66683
```

Also, the commit hashes in `TRAVIS_COMMIT_RANGE` are 12 chars instead of the full 40 as we've seen in pull request builds (this is also different than the `--pretty=%h` length, which is 7).

----

As for the state of the `git` checkout in each, the PR is checked out at [`FETCH_HEAD`][4] which is just the result of running

```
$ git fetch origin +refs/pull/2309/merge:
$ git checkout -qf FETCH_HEAD
```

while the "push" build is checked out at a detached `HEAD` for the lead commit in the branch (c274f02461c82627cc10a71185df46e6091f97a8). This is done explicitly

```
$ git checkout -qf c274f02461c82627cc10a71185df46e6091f97a8
```

In either case, there is only one `git` remote (I was curious in a pull request if the originating fork could be found anywhere).

Finally, a limited checkout is in place, making sure `master` is the only fetched branch in the PR build and `dont-merge-2277-sleuthing` is the only branch in the "push" build:

```
$ git clone --depth=50                                    ...
$ git clone --depth=50 --branch=dont-merge-2277-sleuthing ...
```

There are databases that support [nested transactions](http://docs.oracle.com/cd/E17076_02/html/gsg_txn/C/nestedtxn.html) so it is not all the inconcievable that some user might expect cloud spanner to behave in the same way. Before this check, they would start using nested transactions and nothing would fail since as far as spanner is concerned these are independent transactions. They might incorrectly assume that this is actually working as a nested transaction. That is why it is worthwhile to have this check even if it does not work in all cases.

pex also supports installing requirements from a directory containing a `setup.py` (e.g. `cd ~/dev/project && pex .`). it just doesn't have a special affordance for "editable" dependencies atm because fundamentally our aim has always been repeatable, self contained builds without side effects. so `pex .` nets you effectively the same thing as `pip install -e .`, except instead of being able to edit the code on the fly in say a running webserver you'd have to rebuild the pex each time you run it to encapsulate new changes.

thus, I believe the analog here is simply: `pex -r requirements.txt .` - just run more frequently.

For the normal workflow outside of `node-install`, the only product we expose (caveat) is the node_path for `resolve`. The `node_path` is a mapping of targets and their resolved chroots in `.pants.d`. The resolved chroots is where the target has been "installed" with the node_modules. We don't expect changes in this directory, but is not guaranteed to be a perfect match due to lifecycle scripts, but generally this should be cacheable. The caveat is for bundled node packages. For bundled node packages, we only accept the location of an output_dir.

Hi Daniel,

thanks for bringing this up!
Let me try to answer your questions and bring up a couple of suggestions to discuss.

Bucketing is only a problem, if the metadata sequence length is different to the input sequence length, as then the bucket key would need to be made 3-dimensional: (source length, metadata length, target length). In the case of word-level features, such as POS tags, bucketing can remain as is. Does your use case with GCNs fall into the latter category?

An alternative to modifying the encoder interface to allow for additional metadata, could be to make the source input tensor to the Embedding encoder 3-dimensional: batch, time, channels, where channel represents the word sequence, and the others metadata or word-level features. Similarly, the label tensor would have an additional dimension.
This would keep the interface clean and unchanged, but would only work if the additional features are only required in the embedding (which would concatenate the separate embeddings into a single vector, to be passed into the RNN encoders).

Regarding data I/O, I think it would make sense to build something similar to what OpenNMT does (http://opennmt.net/OpenNMT/data/word_features/) and append features directly to tokens in the input data with a special separator token. One would then also make sure to share that I/O logic in the inference/translate CLI.

We are happy to discuss this in more detail in this issue!

Look at this:

```python


def uri_put_file(creds, uri, fp, content_encoding=None, conn=None):
    # Per Boto 2.2.2, which will only read from the current file
    # position to the end.  This manifests as successfully uploaded
    # *empty* keys in S3 instead of the intended data because of how
    # tempfiles are used (create, fill, submit to boto).
    #
    # It is presumed it is the caller's responsibility to rewind the
    # file position, and since the whole program was written with this
    # in mind, assert it as a precondition for using this procedure.
    assert fp.tell() == 0

    k = _uri_to_key(creds, uri, conn=conn)

    if content_encoding is not None:
        k.content_type = content_encoding

    k.set_contents_from_file(fp, encrypt_key=True)
    return k
```

Note how `content_encoding` is re-interpreted as `content_type`, which is actually correct. So, while the effect is right, the name is wrong.

How about you go ahead and fix the naming?

    def __init__(self, idx):

        self._multiprocess_registry = multiprocess.MultiProcessCollector(self._registry, self._tempdir)

    def shutdown(self):
        # remove the temporary directory

Yay, we managed to meet up! Summarizing:

The problem with the freeimage license is that it is based on version 1 of the MPL (Mozilla Public License), which is incompatible with the GPL. As written on [this page](https://www.gnu.org/licenses/license-list.en.html): *"[The CDDL license] is a free software license. It has a weak per-file copyleft (like version 1 of the Mozilla Public License) which makes it incompatible with the GNU GPL. This means a module covered by the GPL and a module covered by the CDDL cannot legally be linked together. "*

The MPL 2.0 does *not* have this problem, as is written in the [MPL v2.0 FAQ](https://www.mozilla.org/en-US/MPL/2.0/FAQ/): *"Q14: May I combine MPL-licensed code and (L)GPL-licensed code in the same executable program? --- Yes, by creating a "Larger Work" under the terms of Section 3.3. In particular, three requirements must be met: [...]"*

The plan is to contact the FreeImage authors to hopefully convince them to upgrade to the MPL 2.0 license.

Regardless of  what the outcome of that will be, we'll add a plugin based on Pillow, which seems to come really close nowadays in terms of supported formats (and is on GitHub, has CI, etc.). The outcome will likely affect what we'll do about what the default plugin will be for certain formats, and whether we include/depend on FreeImage and Pillow by default.

# Quarterly releases
My proposal for this is to switch to regular, quarterly releases. We define release months and aim for a release in the first half of that month. Whatever feature / change is ready until then gets merged and shipped. What is not ready does not delay the release, and waits for the next one.

What we would need
- [ ] a release schedule
- [ ] other volunteers for doing the releases, as I might not always be available
- [ ] possibly a review of the [release procedure](http://docs.python-soco.com/en/latest/development/release-howto.html)

In case anybody else comes across this post after searching for SoCo discover() returnng None, I too struggled with this for a while. It used to work fine with me, until I made some network changes including changing my router. It turns out that my new router struggles with UDP multicast packets to wireless devices. So if all your Sonos devices are wireless it's worth plugging one in to an ethernet port if you can and then run a discover again. 

This worked for me, although having a Sonos device permanently plugged in is not really a suitable solution for me at the moment. But I can make do without runnning discover() so not a big deal.

Have updated list above to show Sonos have a mix of base 0 and base 1.
If we want to match the Sonos protocol we need to change ```play_from_queue``` and ```remove_from-queue``` to be base 1.
To create our own pythonic standard of base 0 we would need to change 6 functions. 

Obviously any change would not be backward compatible!

The issue here is that the `AWSScout2/rules/data/ip-ranges/aws-in-us.json` path variable gets passed to opinel's `read_ip_ranges()` method in `AWSScout2/rules/rule.py` L107, which then call's opinel's `load_data()` which does the following:
```
            src_dir = os.getcwd()
            src_file = os.path.join(src_dir, data_file)
```
`os.getcwd()` will take the folder from which Scout2 is being executed from (in this case `/root/`), and concatenate the `AWSScout2/rules/data/ip-ranges/aws-in-us.json`  path, resulting in an invalid full path.

When you're getting a non-empty report, that is a Zero Impression Report. The date field is a segment, which implicitly filters zero impression rows. That is why you're seeing an empty report when you include the Date field.

You can infer that the 'model-' prefix is needed b/c client.Entity.__init__ takes a "tag". Prefixes for the different tag types (and how to make a tag from an entity name) can be found in https://github.com/juju/names.

We should fix it correctly using something like:

if not config or 'authorized-keys' not in config:

ok, so it's a good news bad news situation. Yes, both Windows and Linux support LRECL=1G but default to 32K. However, I can't just hard code that, which would just make this all go away. The reason for that is that wx, the I/O subsystem for filename, need to allocate buffer(s) for this file, and if we say a line can be a G then it's trying to allocate at least that, maybe a couple rows worth of buffer. It's not uncommon for MEMSIZE to be set to 1 or 2 G for a workspace server, or any SAS session. When that's the case, the allocation for the buffer will fail and this still won't work. 

This file is used to stream ODS output, data for sd2df, and the CSV ouput for the _CSV version of sd2df. It's allocated up front and reused for the life of the session.

So, what I can do is bump it up to a significantly bigger size, but still within reasonable size as to not run out of memory. The file's deleted and reopened new each time it's used, so the buffers go away and there shouldn't be any real issue. But, having a hard coded value I don't like (kinda have one now at 32k, but ...). So, I can default it to a better size, maybe 1M, but I'll need to add and LRECL key for the IOM access method so this can be tuned and set to whatever anyone ends up needing it to be. 

I see in your workspace server log that your max length for a record in this case may be LRECL=131068.
I'm not positive, but it looks like maybe proc export calculated that. Can't be sure from the log, but it seems plausible.

 I've built a new jar (this file is allocated in the java client). It has 1M hardcoded; to try out. I'll add the option and get it passed through and do this all right, once we verify this works without issue for you.

[saspyiom.zip](https://github.com/sassoftware/saspy/files/1891662/saspyiom.zip)

Can you point your classpath (in your config definition in your sascfg) to this saspyiom.jar instead of the one you are using now (put it wherever you like on your client) and see if this get's your data over to the data frame and runs everything without issue. I'll work on the rest of the fix, but let's be sure this gets you running.

I don't care as much to argue about *which* documentation system to use (though I do subjectively prefer Sphinx), but almost any modern editor is able to collapse (or even auto-collapse) docstrings. As a consequence, I tend to see the "bloat" counter-argument as a weak one.

As @nir0s says, you do get some maintainability benefits, and you also pick up on nice features like being able to cross-reference other modules/classes/functions in the docs (:py:class:`somemodule.Class`). You can still do conversational/tutorial style docs to complement your API reference pages, too.

@ankurankan I ran the same code and got the same error.Moreover, if you run the same code repetitively then sometimes the error doesn't appear. After digging deeper into it , i think i found the bug.
The bug is actually here https://github.com/pgmpy/pgmpy/blob/dev/pgmpy/models/DynamicBayesianNetwork.py#L515
When we call `initialize_initial_state` method, it creates 2 variables `parents` and `evidence_card` .
The value of `parents` is created by calling `self.get_parents(temp_var)` .

Now if we look into the `get_parents` method of `DirectedGraph`  then it returns a list of parents of the queried node by calling `predecessors` method of `nx.Digraph`  over the queried node. This returns a list which is unordered with respect to our need and that's why sometimes we get to see the error.

For example,
1. Following are the values we get internally when we get no `error` -
temp_var ::  ('A', 1)
parents ::  [('B', 1), ('C', 1)]
evidence_card = cpd.cardinality[:0:-1] ::  [2 3]
('B',1) has cardinality 2 and ('C',1) has cardinality 3. That's why `check_model` doesn't throw any error in this case. 

2. Following are the values we get internally when we get `error`-
temp_var ::  ('A', 1)
parents ::  [('C', 1), ('B', 1)]
evidence_card = cpd.cardinality[:0:-1] ::  [2 3]

If you agree then i will try to send a PR soon .

Ok, I now understand better what's going on.  In standard python logging, you cannot use str.format-style braces (i.e. `{msg}: {traceback}`) in log messages.  Only positional arguments are supported.  So my loggers play nicely as far as emitting the pretty colors and styles I have configured outside of txaio; but the messages do not get values interpolated -- hence only seeing `{msg}: {traceback}` in my logs.

The `txaio.aio._TxaioFileHandler` stream handler has a custom `emit()` method that does not behave like standard python loggers and *does* interpolate using str.format().

So my recommendation would be to either use %s type formatters in log messages or to simply call .format()  on the string and not leave it to the log handler/LogRecord to do the interpolation.  Hopefully that makes sense?

            await asyncio.sleep(1)

    def onMessage(self, payload, isBinary):
        if isBinary:
            print("Binary message received: {0} bytes".format(len(payload)))
        else:
            print("Text message received: {0}".format(payload.decode('utf8')))

    async def onClose(self, wasClean, code, reason):
        print("WebSocket connection closed: {0}".format(reason))
        # await asyncio.sleep(delay=.1)
        self.x = 'x' * 50000
        loop = get_event_loop()
        factory = WebSocketClientFactory(u"ws://127.0.0.1:9000")
        factory.protocol = MyClientProtocol
        coro = loop.create_connection(factory, '127.0.0.1', 9000)
        loop.create_task(coro=coro)


if __name__ == '__main__':

    factory = WebSocketClientFactory(u"ws://127.0.0.1:9000")
    factory.protocol = MyClientProtocol

    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    coro = loop.create_connection(factory, '127.0.0.1', 9000)
    loop.run_until_complete(coro)
    loop.run_forever()
    loop.close()
```

```python
elmo = Elmo(requires_grad=False, vocab_to_cache=vocab_to_cache, ...)
for p in elmo._elmo_lstm._elmo_lstm.parameters():
    p.requires_grad = True

Indeed, it looks like writing OSD and monitor to the same hard disk results in some level of seek thrashing. Since seek time with the SSD is significantly reduced the performance gain is nontrivial.

I have minimal experience with tuning ceph. Fantastic articles by @leseb on Ceph IO patterns: [the good], [the bad], and [the ugly] indicate that in addition to performance penalty incurred by running [OSD && (monitor || metadata server)] off a single disk (to be addressed in #3124) it seems placement of the OSD journal matters as well.

It seems to that the ideal would be two physical disks per OSD. But it also seems that moving the OSD's journal to the beginning of the hard disk using a raw partition does help as well. This is exceedingly difficult to do on many cloud providers.

Another option here might just be to require a GCE persistent SSD pool instead of an HDD pool. A 375GB persistent SSD pool on GCE is documented to deliver 10^4 IOPS while the ephemeral local ssd documented in this PR is documented to deliver 10^5 IOPS. Following coreos/bugs#238 two local SSDs should be able to provide 10^6 IOPS over NVMe if work is equally balanced.

By comparison, the default 32GB Persistent Disk this PR replaces was delivering 10^1 write and 10^0 read IOPS at $1.29 per provisioned disk. A 64GB Persistent SSD would at least deliver 10^3 IOPS at $10.88 per provisioned disk.

[the good]: http://www.sebastien-han.fr/blog/2014/02/10/ceph-io-patterns-the-good/
[the bad]: http://www.sebastien-han.fr/blog/2014/02/17/ceph-io-patterns-the-bad/
[the ugly]: http://www.sebastien-han.fr/blog/2014/02/24/ceph-io-patterns-the-ugly/

So, immediately after scaling, I see this:

```
[kent@mbp example-go]$ deis scale web=200
Scaling processes... but first, coffee!
done in 94s
=== jicama-jamboree Processes

--- web: 
web.1 up (v2)
... omitted for brevity...
web.100 up (v2)
```

This shows me 100 processes, even though I just scaled to 200.  For anyone who doesn't know about limits, this looks very odd.  I see the same result as above when using `deis ps`.

It seems a lot of clarity could be added by having the output resemble something like this:

```
[kent@mbp example-go]$ deis scale web=200
Scaling processes... but first, coffee!
done in 94s
=== jicama-jamboree Processes (100 of 200)

--- web: 
web.1 up (v2)
... omitted for brevity...
web.100 up (v2)
```

That sort of `(m of n)` info could be generally useful and insightful for other queries as well.  e.g. `deis apps` or `deis users`.  In the case of `deis ps`, it would actually be possible to also summarize the status of the processes with an additional line that shows the count in each status-- how many are up, how many are down, etc., but that seems like it's probably not totally necessary.  If we're at least making sure users _know_ they're seeing only a fragment of the results, they can at least choose to query again with a higher limit and maybe `grep` it themselves for whatever it is they are looking for.

This improvement isn't likely to happen for Deis v1 (although pull requests are of course welcome). Instead, see the current state-of-the-art testing for Deis v2 at https://github.com/deis/workflow-e2e/issues.

Perhaps this could be a flag in the `connect()` function, for example:
```python
conn = turbodbc.connect(dsn='...', row_type=turbodbc.TUPLE)  # rows would be returned as tuples
conn = turbodbc.connect(dsn='...', row_type=turbodbc.LIST)  # rows would be returned as lists
conn = turbodbc.connect(dsn='...', row_type=turbodbc.ORDERED_DICT)  # rows would be returned as OrderedDicts
conn = turbodbc.connect(dsn='...', row_type=turbodbc.NAMED_TUPLE)  # rows would be returned as NamedTuples
```
Then, any cursors generated from that connection would inherit the row type, but that too could be overridden:
```python
conn = turbodbc.connect(dsn='...', row_type=turbodbc.LIST)
crsr1 = conn.cursor()  # rows from this cursor would be returned as lists
crsr2 = conn.cursor(row_type=turbodbc.ORDERED_DICT)  # rows from this cursor would be returned as OrderedDicts
```
Furthermore, perhaps the user could provide a class to the row type parameter that would do any custom transformation. The class would just have to have a constructor method, say `make()`:
```python
class MyClass():
    def make(seq):
        return list(seq)  # trivial example to always return a list

conn = turbodbc.connect(dsn='...', row_type=MyClass)
```

@pipermerriam 

> New `BaseVM` class with common/shared methods between `VM` and `ShardVM`.

I’m looking into this, it seems easy to drag out some functions that related to gas_limit and uncles to be `VM`-only. But on the other side, so many functions are using **block** as their prefix, suffix, or are related to `block_class` and I’m not sure which of them could be reused in `ShardVM` at this moment.

I suggest that we can start with a simple overriding at the beginning, and then
implement the new logic with **collation** in Stage 2:
1. In `base.py`, rename `VM` to `BaseVM`.
2. Create `vm.py`, `VM`, simply inherit `BaseVM`:
    ```python
    class VM(BaseVM):
        pass
    ```
3. Create `shard_vm.py`, also inherit `BaseVM`:
    ```python
    class ShardVM(BaseVM):
        pass
    ```
4. Rewire the `ShardingVM` fork to inherit this new `ShardVM`:
    ```python
    ShardingVM = ShardVM.configure(
        name='ShardingVM',
        # classes
        _block_class=ShardingBlock,
        _state_class=ShardingVMState,
        # implementations from other VM forks
        create_header_from_parent=staticmethod(create_frontier_header_from_parent),
        compute_difficulty=staticmethod(compute_frontier_difficulty),
        configure_header=configure_frontier_header,
        ....
    )
    ```

5. When we start to apply `Collation` as the container,  we override the basic functions in `ShardVM` if we need.
6. Refactor and clean up the common/shared methods.


I guess it would be less pain of backporting by this way. Any advice and suggestions will be greatly appreciated.

OK, I pushed a fix for this. You should really consider what it is you want to do here. The options `--all` and `--file` don't really make sense together.  One says to take all the packages that are currently installed and reinstall them (which, by the way conda works, will update them). The other says to take the packages and versions from a file and install those. Furthermore, `--no-deps` means that if an update for a package that has a new dependency, it won't be installed. 

The fix I pushed for this causes this to give an error (`Error: No packages found in current osx-64 channels matching: glib 2.43.0 2`), so it's not going to wipe your problems away. You need to consider:

- What are you trying to do?
- If what you want to do really does involve using `--file`, why do you have packages in your file that don't exist in your channels (`glib 2.43.0 2`)? 
- Why are you using `--no-deps`? While that's not strictly related to this bug, it's just asking for trouble down the line. Conda tells you what new packages are installed in a separate column from updated packages. It's easy to review them at install time (especially since you are using `--dry-run` anyway).

That makes sense, but let's unpack this.  The main motivating thing here is that Conda doesn't know where packages that were installed from a channel came from.  What's the situation where that is important?  Checking updates comes to mind.  Something like this could potentially fail:

```console
$ conda install -c tswicegood foobar
# I update foobar to v2
$ conda update foobar # <-- fails
```

Is that right?  Can we tackle that problem at the conda level?  `conda update` could look at the current packages that are being updated and check to see if any of them are from specific channels, then add those unless `-c` was specified in the update.

Admitting that my own workflow is probably biasing my thoughts here I'm :-1: on this change as it exists.  I love the idea of Conda being more aware of where a package came from and being intelligent about interacting with those packages later on, but don't like the idea of adding channels to the config at install time. Historically we didn't provide any compilers, then we provided old ones but it our R packages didn't use the those old ones instead relying on system ones. That brings it's own problems (I don't know how capable your system ones are and anything you build with them will likely not work on other systems).

Forcing our compilers on everyone who installs R on Linux might be the best plan though, but actually upstream R is moving towards providing prebuilt binaries (at least for Windows and macOS).

The other complication is that you are better off not using `install.packages()` for most things since we already provide prebuilt binary conda packages for a lot of the most popular R packages, so conda install r-something is better.

I am considering to patch install.packages so that it tries a conda package first, and failing that, installs compilers if necessary before running the normal codepath.

Overall it's very difficult to know what is best here!

This and #6999 are due to the removal of double quotes in 50764ff262b979529855cced7cdeaa3a0972f51a. 

I spent a while trying to figure out why, but the quotes themselves are a red herring. The problem is that the batch file is being changed by conda **while it is still running**, and the removal of 4 quote characters is affecting the file pointer.

Briefly: when it hits CALL, the batch file parser pushes onto the call stack only its position into the file, and if the number of bytes before the currently executed line changes, you will get very strange results. More info here: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/906586/changing-a-batch-file-when-its-running

This could affect any changes to conda.bat in the future. Maybe a temporary batch file could be created before updating conda?

Is there a difference between pip and non-pip install as to whether they run ./configure before make?  Htslib ships with a Makefile that works even if configure hasn't been run, but that default Makefile declares HAVE_LZMA and so attempts to use the system lzma.h.  The configure script detects this and if not defined it'll use an internal stub file declaring the few symbols it needs.

Hence without the dev package installed, "make" will fail while "./configure;make" will not.

If non-pip isn't using configure then this is an error as the configure script also enables other important features, such as support for https (needed for remote CRAM references from EBI).

I'm reviewing your code and it does not look like you are creating an index before you index the documents. 

When you use a "custom" analyzer for ingestion you need specify the analyzer when you create the index or in a template that well be used when the index is created. 

Please refer to the elastic docs for using [custom analyzers](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/current/analysis-custom-analyzer.html)

as I said before you can either create the index beforehand and then index the documents into that index. 
OR you can use a [template](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/current/indices-templates.html) which will apply settings to any index created whos name matches a particular pattern.

Until this is fixed, here's a workaround I use for reading secrets using `bash` and [`jq`](https://stedolan.github.io/jq/):
```bash
# this prints all the secrets in the keyvault
VAULT_NAME=my-key-vault
for i in `az keyvault secret list --vault-name $VAULT_NAME | jq -r '.[]|.id' | cut -d'/' -f 5` 
do 
  az keyvault secret show --vault-name $VAULT_NAME --name $i 
done | jq '.id,.value'
```

@derekbekoe the problem with defaulting the priority is that you can't have two inbound rules with the same priority. In essence, it would work the first time and then fail the second. Like `nsg rule create` it will only accept a single port or a range of ports. It will not accept a list (comma separated or otherwise). 

I had an implementation that took a space separated list and simply created multiple rules, but then you immediately run into the problem of priority. Without a good heuristic to go by, I stuck with only creating one rule with each call and making priority required.
Actually, it works if value is Non,e but not if the ndoe is not present at all.

Thanks for creating a clean reproduction of the humanoid using TensorFlow!

At a first glance, biggest issue is you are using torque control, which very easily leads to 'explosions' due to insufficient damping. Position control or velocity control will be much more stable and easier (which is what we are doing for most robots). Our MuJoCo importer doesn't load or apply damping. You can easily introduce some damping force by using a non-zero force when setting the VELOCITY_CONTROL line. This is similar to the MuJoCo implicit damping (although using a velocity motor instead of modifying the mass matrix).


dampingForce = 1
for j in range( p.getNumJoints(human) ):
		...
		p.setJointMotorControl2(human, j, controlMode=p.VELOCITY_CONTROL, force=dampingForce)

Oks, once the pipelining has been simplified using a naive implementation we can move forward to the next steps. @fafhrd91 you suggested that we might get rid of the `StreamWriter` once we have removed the complexity under the hood, my suggestion heads to remove the big fish, the `PayloadWriter`.

Right now Aiohttp needs at least the following three layers to handle the response an instance of `StreamWriter`, an instance of `PayloadWriter` and finally an instance of `StreamResponse`, also derivates of the last two ones are allowed. The idea is to reduce these three layers to just the `StreamResponse` one. My gut feeling says that it's doable that would be done making the following changes:

1) Get rid of the superfluous `StreamWriter` and turn the TCP tunning operations as simple functions.
2) Create an `AbstractResponse` as a replacement of the `AbstractPayloadWriter` specifying the functions that must be implemented. Basically, write methods.
3) Move the current logic of the write methods that are in the `PayloadWriter` to the current write methods of `StreamResponse`.
4) The `StreamResponse` - once is called the `prepare` method - will use the transport rather than use the `PaylodWriter`. 
5) Derivates as web file response u others must implement only one derivate of the `StreamResponse` class.

This would be the sketch more or less, surely I will face problems that right now I cant see them. Anyway, thoughts @fafhrd91 and @asvetlov ?

Yup, the absence of proper 'Save As' is painful. Rename, which there's not necessarily a reason for you to know, is an operation that moves the file that's already on-disk. In general, there's no decent handling for having one file opened multiple times in the notebook.

If you do find yourself in this situation where you have one file open multiple times and you want to keep both versions, you can save one version, make a copy of it, then write the second version to the original location.

1. <tab1> save
1. <tab1> make a copy (opens <tab3> with a copy of notebook 1)
1. <tab1> close
1. <tab2> save

In your example, you haven't lost any data until you close tab (7), because after renaming in tab (8), you can press save again in tab (7), and the original notebook will be written again.

>  isn't the server negotiating the protocol version ? In which case other clients that talk to the kernel via the server may be wrong, in which case bumping the version number is not useful ?

I was just looking into this, and the protocol version comes from the javascript, so it's currently identifying as 5.0 and bumping the version in the js to 5.2 should do everything right, as far as I can tell. If this weren't the case, I think the extra key would probably be a more appropriate band-aid.

The Python frontends, on the other hand, are importing the version from jupyter_client right now, so protocol version info will not correctly signal changes in the application after an upgrade to jupyter_client. But in general, the version should be coming from the individual applications, not the defaults in jupyter_client, so we can and should fix this in jupyter_console and qtconsole. But these frontends also don't have the issue on Python 3, so it's of less immediate importance. The answer to unicode issues on Python 2 has always been to upgrade to Python 3 :).

@dslemusp 

Here's a one character fix for your code: use `Omega` instead of `\Omega` for your capital omega. Mostly a coincidence that this fixes it, but it does.

I tracked this down, and it's a doozy. The first thing to note is that it's the plain text (unicode) output, not the latex output that's causing the problem. The second point is that the immediate cause is a bug in Sympy's unicode output, where it allows splitting lines in between a character and its combining characters (dot in this case), which results in the text trying to put the dot on a newline. The third bit is that only Safari seems to be affected, due to an unconditional unicode normalization.

## Gruesome details:

A minimal test to reproduce this:

```python
print(u'\n\u0307x')
```

Code point [307](http://www.fileformat.info/info/unicode/char/0307/index.htm) is "combining dot above", and the issue at hand is that it's appearing after a newline. The utterly bizarre source of the problem is that Safari normalizes text to its combined format (NFC, see [this webkit bug](https://bugs.webkit.org/show_bug.cgi?id=113001)) when submitting ajax requests, so the JSON characters:

    \, n, DOT, x

become:

    \, N WITH A DOT, x

both of which look like:

    \ṅx

The NFC form that webkit sends is invalid JSON because the `\n` escape is no longer present.

[This notebook](https://nbviewer.jupyter.org/gist/minrk/24327a18b5b3bff746194962f40636fd) illustrates what's going on.

## What we can do about it

I don't see a way to tell webkit to knock it off, so there are two options I can see:

1. use ascii-safe JSON.stringify on Safari
2. normalize to NFD server-side before parsing with JSON (optionally only after failed parse, since this should be rare)

I'm not thrilled about either one, especially for such a narrow case. Since it does require truly bizarre sequence of events to hit it.

> The behaviour is pretty inconsistent, I'm not totally sure why, because when I tried to download a changed copy of the PDF a few minutes later, it updates correctly.

That does sound like browser caching. The browser can keep a copy of data it gets from the server, to save time if it needs to get the same data again later. But then you need ways to decide when the data has changed, so that the browser can get the updated data. The simple way to do that is to have a time limit. A cleverer way is [ETags](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HTTP_ETag), which let the browser ask for 'give me the latest version if it's not this one that I've already got'.

* create pre-signed URL for each chunk
* send each chunk to swift using the URL
* complete upload

> Yes, that's right. There's a graph to draw here, but I don't have your
> ascii art skills. For chain-structured functions, have three chains
> involved here: the top one is from the function eval, the middle one from
> the first call to tf.gradients, and the bottom one from the second call
> to tf.gradients. As you say, the bottom one doesn't actually depend on
> the values from the middle one, by linearity. Both the bottom chain and the
> middle chain depend on the values of the top chain.
>
> Even better, there won't even be extra memory overhead at runtime: because
> the top chain and bottom chain have arrows pointing in the same direction
> (i.e. to the right), and the arrows from the top chain to the bottom don't
> skip steps, the TF graph executor is smart enough to reuse memory as it
> performs the evaluation, rather than evaluating things in a bad order and
> keeping too large an active set.
>
> One way to convince yourself that g isn't evaluated at runtime is the

We should definitely find something like this that works. The wrinkle is that when you're fetching a specific revision, we have to know how far back in history that revision is. A simple strategy might be something like "clone all repos at depth 1, but if we ever hit an error trying to find a ref, do a complete history fetch before giving up". That would be nice and fast for projects that don't specify a revision at all, but I worry it would be slower for the common case (or what I _hope_ is the common case) where you do specify the revision. In that case you're almost guaranteed to need the history fetch, but now you'd at least be paying the cost of a second set of round trips.

Back when I looked into this last, I didn't see any git feature like "fetch only enough history to reach commit xyz," but if such a thing existed it would immediately solve this problem :)

You're right. I hadn't fully considered the implications of making it async. It **would** make usage of the sdk more difficult for >50% of use-cases. As an aside, I believe that python3's async api hasn't changed since 3.5, and it's considered stable in 3.6, but that's beside the point.

The reason I opened this issue is that it would be nice to include a lot of the architecture needed for building an event-driven app in one place. One of the more obvious examples would be queuing requests as I believe is done in the JS sdk. Maybe there just needs to be another package wrapping this one providing that async architecture; I don't know.

At the very least, to support async use-cases, we need to get rid of the `while` loop inside of `_send` or make it explicit in the documentation that the `_send` method must be overridden to support async.

Just a quick summary.

Results of PR #126 and #127:

- We now have made [most parts of the code base](https://travis-ci.org/PyCQA/pylint-django/jobs/338127371#L473) follow PEP8 and other rules enforced by flake8
- We have a build matrix on Travis CI that shows [which checks and tests are failing](https://travis-ci.org/PyCQA/pylint-django) (Python 2.7 mostly for test data, flake8 for code in tests and the `compat` module, Pylint with approx. 80 complaints)
- We're [allowing to fail](https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint-django/blob/master/.travis.yml#L18-L21) the mentioned build jobs in order to get a green build

What we now need is:

1. Someone to look at the **remaining flake8 complaints** (which appear to be dangerous to adjust w/o knowing what the code base does)

Hello @theilluminaughti @brianmay 

I believe sshuttle will only intercept and redirect traffic going out (via firewall rules). 

If there is any traffic going out to some UDP/53 port it will get intercepted and tunneled. However when using `/etc/hosts` the name resolution is happening locally, there is no DNS traffic going out to any UDP/53 port so there is nothing to intercept and tunnel. Note that all other names (not explicitely defined in `/etc/hosts`) will be tunneled.

About DNS tunneling via SOCKS ignoring the `/etc/hosts` file, maybe that is a setting of the application you are using. For instance in Firefox, you can enable that in `about:config`, `network.proxy.socks_remote_dns`.

The only thing I can think of right now to do something like this is to backup `/etc/hosts` file when sshuttle starts, clear it, and restore on exit. This is similar to what is already done when `--auto-hosts` is used. However I cannot see any advantages of doing this. What do you have in mind?

There's currently no API in Python client to query any broker state (there's the metadata() API in C which would do the trick, but it hasn't been ported yet).

But what you can do which I think would serve you quite well is registering an error_cb and look for the _ALL_BROKERS_DOWN error event the first couple of seconds after starting your program.

This is okay to do on startup as some sort of precaution, but since the brokers may actually be valid but currently unreachable for whatever temporary networking reason, you might exit your program wrongfully.
This is why you shouldnt exit if you see _ALL_BROKERS_DOWN later while your program runs, librdkafka will automatically reconnect and resume operation as soon as brokers start coming up again.

The datetime SIG contains discussions of several possible ways to get "timeline arithmetic".  Most popular was to trigger "I want timeline arithmetic" by deriving from a new tzinfo marker subclass.

In any case, it can't be done without changes to the core, short of the kind of approach pytz took, including pytz requiring the user to keep calling `normalize()` after arithmetic.  Note that just adding a new kind of timedelta doesn't solve the problem:  `datetime - datetime` then has no idea whether you want timeline or classic subtraction.

The default cannot change:  classic arithmetic has been the rule since the start, and that was over a dozen years ago.  And Guido still likes that decision.

In the meantime, you "should" either do nothing about arithmetic (that's my advice), or copy what pytz does.  Anything between those will leave you with unique behaviors nobody expects.  People who need timeline arithmetic in std Python now can get it by working in UTC, converting to local time only when necessary.  However, my guess is that `dateutil` will need to implement its own `fromutc()` to handle all the pathologies in the zoneinfo database correctly.  As documented, the default `fromutc()` is _not_ ambitious.

Note that no changes are required in the core to do correct UTC -> local conversions in all cases (provided you implement `fromutc()`).  It's only the other direction that's an intractable problem in some cases (no way to disambiguate local times in a fold).  The PEP is a solution to that.  Before then, blame Python!  It is, after all, Python's fault ;-)

@alexsilva @zhang20054460 @psa-jforestier Am I correct in my assessment that this *only* occurs on Python 2.7 on Windows and it is only a warning (i.e. it has no effect on the code)?

For some reason I can't get `unittest.mock` to work properly for this, but here's a MWE:

```python
from dateutil.parser import parse

def test_parser_warning():
    import time
    old_tzname = time.tzname
    mocked_tznames =  (b'Paris, Madrid', b'Paris, Madrid (heure d\x92\xe9t\xe9)')
    time.tzname = mocked_tznames
    try:
        parse('2017-01-09T13:13:15CLA')
    finally:
        time.tzname = old_tzname

test_parser_warning()
```

Honestly, if this is a warning only and it exists only on Python 2.7, I'm inclined to just leave it as is. The problem as I see it:

1. I'm fairly certain that the parser already can't properly tokenize long time zone names (all the ones I see here have spaces, and those are not going to match `res.tzname` *anyway*), so we don't actually *gain* anything by fixing this.
2. This is only a warning, and it only ever occurs on Python 2.7 - presumably this is because Python 3 knows how to pull your `tzname` as properly-encoded unicode. If seeing it bothers you, wrap the `parse` call in a context that ignored `UnicodeWarning`.
3. As far as I can tell, there is no way for me to figure out what encoding is being used for the `tzname` strings. Probably `utf-8` works in most cases, but there's a good chance that I might infer the wrong encoding in some cases, and this will actually suppress the warning *despite the comparison being wrong*.

I don't think there's any particular need to bend over backwards to prevent warnings from showing up on Python 2.7 here.

Please let me know if my assessment is wrong here and this actually *is* a bigger problem than I think.

Okay here's a funny mistake:

My real test_data.csv is much bigger. It comprises all postal code areas of germany.
In east germany there is a big region of postal codes **starting with a leading 0**.

Guess what -> that exact region doesn't get any color from the legend in the upper right corner. It gets the default color of folium instead.

**Here's why**:

Consider this one:
```
plz,anzahl
"01097",50
```

It will be read by this line from the folium tutorial:
`plz_data = pd.read_csv(r'test_data.csv')`

At this step, the leading 0 gets already dropped. Thus my solution from earlier doesn't work, because
`plz_data['plz'] = plz_data['plz'].astype(str)`
will now only convert the int 1097 to '1097'. But we need '01097'.

The solution is to not drop the leading zeros at the **input level** like this:
`plz_data = pd.read_csv(r'test_data.csv', converters={'plz': lambda x: str(x)})`

But that means we should not add a conversion somewhere inside folium. Because it doesn't help if there are leading zeros. Maybe we should just update the documentation instead, stating that the key column should be converted to string via the converters argument of pandas' read_csv function?

The `*` trigger has the lowest matching priority, so it only matches the user's message when there was no better trigger that matched it. The bot sorts all the triggers internally (regardless of their location in the source `.rive` files) in a "most specific first" ordering, where longer/more specific triggers are tested first.

For example if you started with this:

```
+ hello
- Hello human!

+ *
- I didn't understand that.
```

A user saying "hi" instead of "hello" would get the "I didn't understand that." reply. But as soon as you make it so a trigger would match "hi":

```
+ (hello|hi)
- Hello human!

- *
- I didn't understand that.
```

Now "hi" would get the "Hello human!" response.

import java.nio.ByteBuffer
import java.io.ByteArrayOutputStream

import com.intel.analytics.bigdl.dataset._
import com.intel.analytics.bigdl.dataset.image._

import scala.collection.mutable.ArrayBuffer

def loadBinaryFile(filePath: String): Array[Byte] = {
  val files = sc.binaryFiles(filePath)
  val bytes = files.first()._2.toArray()
  bytes
}

def load(featureFile: String, result : ArrayBuffer[ByteRecord]): Unit =
 {
    val rowNum = 32
    val colNum = 32
    val imageOffset = rowNum * colNum * 3 + 1
    val channelOffset = rowNum * colNum
    val bufferOffset = 8

    val featureBuffer =
    {
      ByteBuffer.wrap(loadBinaryFile(featureFile))
    }

    val featureArray = featureBuffer.array()
    val featureCount = featureArray.length / (rowNum * colNum * 3 + 1)

    var i = 0
    while (i < featureCount)
    {
      val img = new Array[Byte]((rowNum * colNum * 3 + bufferOffset))
      val byteBuffer = ByteBuffer.wrap(img)
      byteBuffer.putInt(rowNum)
      byteBuffer.putInt(colNum)

      val label = featureArray(i * imageOffset).toFloat
      var y = 0
      val start = i * imageOffset + 1
      while (y < rowNum)
      {
        var x = 0
        while (x < colNum)
        {
          img((x + y * colNum) * 3 + 2 + bufferOffset) =
            featureArray(start + x + y * colNum)
          img((x + y * colNum) * 3 + 1 + bufferOffset) =
            featureArray(start + x + y * colNum + channelOffset)
          img((x + y * colNum) * 3 + bufferOffset) =
            featureArray(start + x + y * colNum + 2 * channelOffset)
            x += 1
}
            y += 1
      }
       result.append(ByteRecord(img, label + 1.0f))
       i += 1
    }
}
 def loadTrain(dataFile: String): Array[ByteRecord] =
    {
      val allFiles = Array(
      dataFile + "/data_batch_1.bin",
      dataFile + "/data_batch_2.bin",
      dataFile + "/data_batch_3.bin",
      dataFile + "/data_batch_4.bin",
      dataFile + "/data_batch_5.bin"
      )
      val result = new ArrayBuffer[ByteRecord]()
      allFiles.foreach(load(_, result))
      result.toArray
    }

def loadTest(dataFile: String): Array[ByteRecord] =
{
    val result = new ArrayBuffer[ByteRecord]()
    val testFile = dataFile + "/test_batch.bin"

Constructing explicit hierarchies of preference is actually a question I'm quite often asked. Unfortunately, you are correct in thinking that this would required fairly complicated changes to the Cython code in LightFM: while changing `sample_weights` is an approximation to this, it does not attempt to preserve the rankings you have. (And, as you say, may be of limited effectiveness: it's possible that changing relative weights even more to weigh top movies even more heavily will work, but I am not fully convinced.)

Instead, I would recommend something more flexible, like [Spotlight](https://github.com/maciejkula/spotlight/) or [wyrm](https://github.com/maciejkula/wyrm) (my new deep learning library, with an example of building a recommender [here](https://github.com/maciejkula/wheedle/blob/razzle-dazzle/src/models/factorization.rs)). ```python
import warnings

class PVLibDeprecationWarning(UserWarning):
    pass

message = complicated_stuff_copied_from_matplotlib()
warnings.warn(message, PVLibDeprecationWarning)
```

You could imagine a ``pvlibModelValidationWarning`` defined and used in this simple way. The complicated stuff in #427 is to autofill information from the decorated function, but we'd skip that for this feature (at least initially).

When we want to create a rollup store for polymorphic container (for sure with joined table polymorphism), two problems emerge:
1. The container subquery contain redundant columns (for sure container `id` and base class `id`) that when queried without aliases violate SQL syntax - here I applied `reduce_columns` parameter to remove columns that are redundant and added `with_alias` to apply aliases to columns (this one actually might be sufficient).
2. The order by, taken from the container `_order_by` does not work for base class fields in a current query structure. Let's say we have a base class `Task() { description }` and a container class `SubTask(Task) { priority }`, then when we want to order by `SubTask.description`, the produced SQL would look like `ORDER BY task.description` but the outer 'order by' we produce in rollup store, does not have `task` in `FROM` list - we aliased just a container subquery with `sub_task` name. Thus to avoid this and to preserve reusable `_order_by` from container store I added extra join with a container class.

```
The two functions above are underspecified in case the input
values f, g satisfy eqε,σ (which is only when one is −0 and
the other is +0). This means that we consider as acceptable
models any structures with function families maxε,σ and
minε,σ that satisfy the specifications above, regardless of
whether they return −0 or +0 for (−0, +0), and for (+0, −0).
Although underspecification is inconvenient for implementors,
it is necessary as IEEE-754 itself allows either value to be
returned and compliant implementations do vary. For example,
on some Intel processors the result returned by the x87 and
SSE units is different.
```

This looks technically ok, but I'm not sure it's a good design idea to add those. Like @delcypher said, in IEEE754 extended and "extendable" formats are only a suggestion. The SMT FP standard doesn't include a name for these sorts either. The danger is that users will expect all FP operations in 80-bit will behave like Intel x87 - 80-bit numbers, which is not necessarily the same as using 80-bit IEEE754 numbers or 80-bit SMT numbers. I don't remember the exact details of those differences though; they could be trivial.

Hello all, 

I've sketched some notes on the format for separation logic accepted by CVC4:

http://church.cims.nyu.edu/wiki/Separation_Logic

The format was developed in discussion with Radu Iosif and Cristina Serban at Verimag.  We are interested in discussing possible formats for SL with a larger SMT audience.

The format we currently support in CVC4 differs from the previous proposal is that the "Ref" type constructor is not builtin.  Instead, e.g. the predicate "pto" is fully parametric, e.g. given a fixed background theory T, we fix two sorts "Loc" and "Data" of that theory.  Then, pto is a predicate of sort Loc x Data.  We believe that allowing the separation logic extension to be fully parametric in this way comes with fewer complications and still allows for one to specify problems e.g. where Loc=(Ref T) for some user-defined type constructor Ref.


However, the challenge is how to specify what "Loc" and "Data" are.  There are two possible ways we have considered doing this so far:

(1) Require the solver to infer "Loc" and "Data".  

As a simple example:
 (set-logic QF_ALL_SUPPORTED)
 (declare-const x Int)
 (assert (pto x 0))
 (check-sat)
Upon parsing (pto x 0), the solver infers that Loc=Int, Data=Int. 

CVC4 will throw an error if given pto's of different type:
 (set-logic QF_ALL_SUPPORTED)
 (declare-sort U 0)
 (declare-const x U)
 (assert (and (pto x 0) (pto 1 2)))
 (check-sat)

Requiring the solver to do this inference means that the empty heap constraint "emp" needs to be made unambiguous. To do this, we define emp as a predicate taking two dummy arguments whose types are Loc and Data. As an example:
 (set-logic QF_ALL_SUPPORTED)
 (declare-sort U 0)
 (declare-const x U)
 (assert (emp x 0))
 (check-sat)
Here, (emp x 0) is the empty heap constraint for Loc=U, Data=Int.

(2) Require the user to define "Loc" and "Data".

This could be done e.g. by adding a new smt2 command "define-sl-sorts":
 (set-logic QF_ALL_SUPPORTED)
 (declare-sort U 0)
 (define-sl-sorts U Int)
 (declare-const x U)
 (assert (pto x 0))
 (check-sat)
which sets Loc=U, Data=Int.

The advantage of (2) is that "emp" can be nullary since Loc and Data are established up front, and less burden is put on the solver. However, a disadvantage to (2) is that the operational semantics of the SMT2 command language becomes more complicated:  Before issuing "assert", the user must issue "define-sl-sorts". However, the user may need to issue "declare-sort"/"declare-datatypes" before issuing "define-sl-sorts".  This is complicates the picture in Figure 4.1 on page 49 of http://smtlib.cs.uiowa.edu/papers/smt-lib-reference-v2.5-r2015-06-28.pdf.


At the moment, we have chosen to do (1), although neither is ideal, so we're definitely interested in further discussion of a standard format for SL in SMT.

> I think this is because a nonlinearity layer is returned from `L.batch_norm()` (whereas `DenseLayer` has the nonlinearity as an attribute)?

Exactly, it's because the `batch_norm()` convenience function inserts batch normalization in front of the nonlinearity. Usually you won't call `bn.get_params()` anyway, but `lasagne.layers.get_all_params(bn)` to obtain the parameters of all layers, and then the batch normalization parameters are included.

> But then it's probably easier to overload `border_mode` instead, even if it reads a bit less intuitively.

That was my reasoning for Theano, where we want them to be mutually exclusive (we don't necessarily know the filter shape at compile time, so combining custom padding with anything else than a valid convolution would complicate matters), and the easiest way to achieve that is by merging them into a single argument.

> Having `border_mode` and `pad` be completely orthogonal doesn't really add any useful functionality.

That's right, it just retains the readibility of the current version, and makes it easier to document/understand than the current version, but at the cost of complicating the code (`pad != 0` would need to check for `border_mode` even if it will hardly ever be combined with `border_mode != 'valid'` in practice).

> `border='same'`, `border=4` both make sense, I like that. Same for `pad='same'`, `pad=4` I guess :)

Same for me! +1 for merging them under a better name. In both cases, the default would be `0`, right? `pad='valid'` as a default wouldn't work that well, `border='valid'` would be an acceptable alternative to `border=0`.
In Theano, it will always be called `border_mode` for backwards compatibility, and `border` would be closer to that, but we don't have to follow Theano.
`pad` is a bit closer to what the border mode does conceptually (it pads the input before applying a valid convolution), but it's very nonstandard and the documentation might be confusing to people looking to do a full convolution ("`pad` controls the amount of zero-padding before applying a (valid) convolution. For a valid convolution, leave it at the default value of zero. For a full convolution, set it to `"full"`. [...]", compared to "`border` controls how the border is treated in applying the convolution. [...]").

> Either of those would be fine.

I'd still like to choose wisely :)

Yes it's not specifically about --from-url; it's that bugzilla.redhat.com has an extension to accept URL style field names (query_format=advanced, and others) over the XMLRPC API. --from-url just decomposes a URL to a python dict to pass to the XMLRPC API. If you break the URL down into individual --field= calls you are largely going to get the same result

Some URL parameters _are_ valid XMLRPC query param names, so it might work for some cases, if you strip out the query_format=advanced bit that is always attached to URL query strings

Now I understand your point @glasserc 
Here's one version using the `OVER ()` technique:
```sql
select COUNT(*) OVER (), id, as_epoch(last_modified) AS last_modified,
MD5(jsonb_pretty(data)) AS data
from records
WHERE parent_id = '/buckets/build-hub/collections/releases'
AND collection_id = 'record'
ORDER BY last_modified DESC
LIMIT 10;
```

Compared to these two:
```sql
select count(*)
from records
WHERE parent_id = '/buckets/build-hub/collections/releases'
AND collection_id = 'record';

select id, as_epoch(last_modified) AS last_modified,
MD5(jsonb_pretty(data)) AS data
from records
WHERE parent_id = '/buckets/build-hub/collections/releases'
AND collection_id = 'record'
ORDER BY last_modified DESC
LIMIT 10;
```

If I break these up and run all three 10 times and record the best possible time with my 183,240 rows database:

1. 955.45ms
2. 99.19ms
3. 1.72ms

Difference is that it's roughly 10x slower to do the `COUNT(*) OVER ()`. /me stunned!
When I attempt the same benchmark but on my larger database (775,557 rows) it blows up:

1. 5179.24ms
2. 758.27ms
3. 1.70ms

To sanity check I compared it with the *original* query (the one still in `master`) and output looks the same: https://gist.github.com/peterbe/21b3e12484cc4937ec002c76f277f2cd

Lastly, the `COUNT(*) OVER ()` is really meant for doing partitioning. For example, a count of something by weekday for example. 

Also, the biggest problem with the `SELECT COUNT(*) OVER (), id, ...` query is that it's using the same WHERE clause for the count as it does for the `LIMIT 10` records retrieval. In particular, according to the unit tests if you filter by including tombstones, the number of records isn't expected to the best same as the "total_count". 

Next, anybody got a really good window function to do the same that I can use?

Why are the 5 blocking calls (in your example) running sequentially to take 25 seconds? I distinctly remember that one of my first patches to netdisco was to parallelize blocking detection, so the entire detection phase would not take longer than the longest blocking call. In your example, it would be 5 seconds. 

This solution seems much better than to decrease the reply time available, which will increase the risk that slow devices (with a wakeup time) might miss it.

If the detection is no longer multithreaded, this seems like a regression and should be fixed as such.

Ok but the smearing I am talking about is different.

I think you and Max both design metrics that are "hard" - i.e., a hard cutoff of how deep into the bands to go or an integer number for the number of significant contributors with a "hard" cutoff on what is considered significant. I tend to design and prefer metrics that are smooth and continuous.

For example, to see how deep into the bands to go, the paper above shows how to do so continuously / gracefully (not just smearing the dos and giving a hard cutoff afterward) where the energies that are closest to the band edge are weighted the most and energies further from the band edge are weighted less in a continuous manner. Similarly, for the number of significant contributors, it would be better to define some kind of entropy metric (e.g., sum of xln(x) over all orbital contributions or something) rather than an integer based on setting some hard threshold for what's "significant".

I'll add a new issue regarding some of the smoothness factors.

> or even "hotdoc should have made their build fail because the file lists were out of sync"

I'm not sure how hotdoc would even be able to know that :)

>  I share @pwithnall's concern here about duplicate file-lists inevitably going out of sync.

I understand it too, and would like it to be addressed the proper way, but : 

* Most autotools projects out there do not use non-recursive automake, and as such the documented file-list will be duplicated in a subdir automake file.
* IMHO, the actual proper way would be to have these file lists defined in configure.ac, which would make them available for subsitution at configure time, and avoid duplication, however this is an (unexplained) limitation of autotools, see https://www.gnu.org/software/automake/manual/html_node/Conditional-Sources.html

> I like @pwithnall's plan of passing the files to be documented as arguments to hotdoc, particularly from a "don't repeat yourself" perspective.

Note that having command-line arguments override whatever is defined in the json file is already supported in hotdoc, however for projects that use recursive automake (aka most of them), 'don't repeat yourself' is not an available option anyway, as noted above.

> If you want a self-contained hotdoc.json as well, then I would suggest writing an augmented version of hotdoc.json, which does include detailed file lists formed from its command-line arguments, into hotdoc's output

That is a solution, but I feel like it's simply a workaround to cater to autotools' deficiencies, and I don't like the idea of making hotdoc more complex for the sake of a specific build system's quirks.

> The human-editable source code hotdoc.json.in is not self-contained anyway, because it has Automake substitutions which need to be substituted before it can be used; and in larger/more complex projects (the ones that really need good documentation), it's quite likely to be documenting generated source files (Automake $(BUILT_SOURCES), etc.) which will need to be compiled before Hotdoc can offer reasonably complete documentation. Given that, there isn't a huge difference between having ./configure generate the hotdoc.json that you want, and having make (indirectly invoking hotdoc) generate it?

The fact that hotdoc.json is or isn't editable by humans depending on the setup used isn't hotdoc's problem, neither is the fact that sources are generated or not.

The difference between the two solutions is that I think autotools' specific design issues should be solved at the autotools level.

Let's look at this issue from another perspective, if autoconf didn't allow defining CFLAGS, we would also need to pass them at make time to hotdoc, however as there's no such limitation, I see it as the right way to pass these, in order to avoid duplication. As the limitation for SOURCES is unfortunately introduced by autotools, I think the price should be paid there, not in hotdoc.

For what it's worth, there is always `CAP_802_15_4_PIB`, which maps the entire 802.15.4 PIB space to the properties between `PROP_15_4_PIB__BEGIN` (1024) and `PROP_15_4_PIB__END` (1280).

*macPromiscuousMode* maps to the 802.15.4 PIB identifier `0x51`. Thus, if a device has the `CAP_802_15_4_PIB` capability, it can use the property `PROP_15_4_PIB_MAC_PROMISCUOUS_MODE` (property id of 1105), which would behave as intended.

I doubt many NCPs will implement that optional capability, but it is nonetheless defined in the protocol.

Given that the above plan isn't going to work for the Windows kernel, let's scrap it and go with plan B.

The plan B involves three pull requests:

1. New pull request with just the API changes, with the API implementations largely ignoring the instance pointer. Everything would still use globals internally. This allows us to review and approve *just* the API changes independently of other changes. It also allows us to go ahead and get started on the work to switch over to the new API without having to worry about destabilizing our own code. Also, let's use `otInstance` instead of `otContext`, since we generally use the term 'context' to refer to a void pointer we can get passed to callbacks.
2. There are some APIs that don't need access to the primary `otInstance` structure since they are actually methods to independent objects. The message pool and crypto APIs come to mind. The changes necessary to support this would happen at this step. This pull request would include API changes and internal changes.
3. Implement internal support for `otInstance`. This pull request would include no API changes.

This approach allows for the library to be usable at each step and amortizes the integration workload over a period of time. This makes life much easier on our end.

I'd suggest that it's quite easy to know if the timeout is reasonable - if the time between calls to the interface layer is short compared to the timeout, then it's too long. The predominant use case of the cache is for calling through the `*py_interface` layers, in which rapid repeated calls to objects can be optimised through caching. If the calls are a relatively rare, there is little to be gained (except for perhaps the unusual case in which there are many objects that are called rapidly with repeats relatively rarely).

Of course, FFTW still has it's wisdom, so the actual time of creation for an FFTW object is quite short, so for rare callings this should be the way object creation is optimised.

That misses the point (and the problem) entirely.

Here's the real issue, quoted from my original bug report.

 > Using tr or similar to work around this is unreliable - there may be
 > actual commas in any of the tab-separated fields.

(I made a mistake there. I meant to say "any of the comma-separated fields", not "tab-separated").

If there are commas inside the fields, in the actual data, it is **impossible** for any other program that post-processes csvkit's output to distinguish between a comma within a field from a comma that separates a field.  That includes csvformat as well as tr, awk, sed, or any other tool.

**THAT** is why being able to specify the output field separator is essential - so you can choose a delimiter which **ISN'T** in the data.

If csvkit can't do that, then it's not fit for purpose.

This sort of data vs delimiter problem is very common.

If you can choose the delimiter, then it's a trivial problem to solve, easily scripted.

If you can't choose, then the **only** way to be certain that your output isn't going to be garbled by an extra comma is to visually inspect it and edit it by hand - it is a task that requires human-level intelligence and great patience and attention to detail (or at least AI parsing of the data)
